examples.text: Yaar, socha tha weekend pe sprint khatam ho jayega, but achanak se 2-3 log period leave le lenge toh deadline kaise meet hoga? Matlab, it's just not fair on others who have to cover.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies menstrual leave is an unfair burden on others and hinders productivity, subtly questioning its legitimacy based on perceived impact on workload.

examples.text: Sunna hai new policy mein period leave mil rahi hai? Matlab, what about us guys? Agar humari bhi tabiyat kharab ho, toh kya special 'man flu' leave milegi? Aise toh unfair hai na.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions the fairness of menstrual leave by demanding an equivalent for men, framing it as preferential treatment and reverse discrimination.

examples.text: On the recent discussions about extended maternity benefits (MBB 2017), while well-intentioned, we must consider the practical implications for startups. A significant investment in training and then a 6-month hiatus, plus the potential for a 2-year childcare leave, puts immense financial strain. It inadvertently makes hiring women in certain age groups a risk for smaller companies, creating an unfair competitive landscape for them.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses economic and practical arguments to frame maternity/childcare leave as a liability for businesses, subtly discouraging hiring women of reproductive age by presenting their reproductive choices as a financial risk.

examples.text: Honestly, while I understand the pain, why are we institutionalizing menstrual leave? It creates an expectation that women will take time off regularly, making us seem less reliable. Men manage their health issues without special 'leave' policies. This isn't about equality; it's about treating women as inherently weaker and giving us special treatment that disadvantages our careers in the long run. We already struggle with perception.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman internalizing neosexist beliefs, arguing against menstrual leave by framing it as 'special treatment' that makes women appear weaker and less reliable, ultimately disadvantaging their careers.

examples.text:
Speaker A (HR Head): "So, for this Senior PM role, what do you think of Reena's profile? She interviewed really well."
Speaker B (Team Lead): "She's good, no doubt. But she just got married a few months back, right? You know how it is. If we onboard her now, and she goes on leave after 6-8 months, that's a whole project cycle disruption. It's tough on the team."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Assumes a newly hired married woman will soon take maternity leave, framing it as a potential disruption and liability for the project and team, impacting hiring decisions.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Colleague): "Did you see Priya today? She was so snappy in the meeting, completely unlike her."
Speaker B (Colleague): "Haan yaar, I noticed. Must be that time of the month, I guess? Happens to all of them. Don't take it personally."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses 'that time of the month' as an explicit, biologically deterministic explanation for a woman's irritability, dismissing her behavior and subtly attributing it to her biology.

examples.text: Seeing so many discussions on #MaternityBenefits. While crucial, does anyone else feel the extended childcare leave (like 2 years for mothers) sometimes creates an unintended career gap that's hard to bridge for others, especially men who also have childcare responsibilities but no such 'official' provision? #FairnessInWorkplace #HRPolicy
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman subtly frames extended childcare leave for mothers as an 'unintended career gap' and 'unfair advantage' when compared to men's lack of similar provisions, invoking reverse discrimination.

examples.text: So #PeriodLeave is gaining traction. Great for some, but what about others? My male colleagues suffer from chronic back pain or migraines, but no one's getting 'back pain leave'. This just feels like special treatment. Why should one biological function get a pass over another? #WorkplaceEquality #NoSpecialTreatment
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions the fairness of menstrual leave by comparing it to other health issues men face, framing it as an unwarranted special privilege for women.

examples.text: My cousin just got promoted to AVP, but he mentioned they were considering a female colleague, Riya, for the same role. They went with him because they figured Riya might be planning a family soon, and they didn't want to risk the position going 'empty' after a few months.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Justifies promoting a male candidate over a female candidate due to an assumption about her potential maternity leave, viewing her reproductive choices as a career impediment and an organizational liability.

examples.text: Look, my daughter just joined an IT firm, and they're talking about period leave. I told her not to take it. People will just think you're weak or can't handle stress. Why give them a reason to think less of you? We women have to work twice as hard to prove ourselves anyway, don't give them an excuse.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman discourages another woman from taking menstrual leave, internalizing and perpetuating the belief that it would mark her as 'weak' and create a negative perception, making it 'special treatment' that hurts women's careers.

examples.text: Dude, our team is already short-staffed, and now three people are out with fever. Project deadline looks impossible. HR really needs to figure out better contingency planning for leaves, be it any kind.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses concern about overall team capacity and project deadlines due to general leaves, without specifically targeting or attributing blame to gender-specific leaves. The focus is on universal HR planning.

examples.text: I strongly support #PeriodLeave, but how can companies ensure it's implemented respectfully and doesn't lead to women being subtly penalized for taking it? We need clear guidelines and cultural shifts, not just policy changes. #WorkplaceEquity
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Supports menstrual leave but raises valid concerns about implementation challenges and potential negative consequences without questioning the legitimacy of the leave itself or framing it as unfair special treatment. Focuses on equitable execution.

examples.text: Yaar, ye period leave ka funda samajh nahi aata. Matlab, humare liye toh kuch nahi hai, na? Company ko bhi toh extra burden hai, aise toh cost badhegi for no reason.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies menstrual leave is unfair 'special treatment' that burdens the company financially, while men get no equivalent benefit.

examples.text: Pooja aaj badi chirchiri ho rahi hai project meeting mein. Lagta hai 'woh din' chal rahe hain uske. Aise toh project deadine miss hogi agar sab aise mood swings dikhayenge.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses menstruation as an implicit, dismissive explanation for a woman's mood/performance dip, linking it to potential project failure.

examples.text: As a woman, I'm conflicted. While I understand the need for menstrual leave, doesn't 26 weeks of paid maternity leave already put a huge financial strain on companies, especially startups? And now this? It just makes hiring young women a bigger 'risk' from a business perspective. We need to think about overall gender equality in promotions too, if women are perceived as less reliable due to such policies.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames maternity and menstrual leave as a business 'risk' and financial strain, subtly arguing against them by invoking productivity and 'overall gender equality' concerns.

examples.text: The push for period leave is ridiculous. What about men? Do we get 'stress leave' for dealing with women's mood swings? Seriously, businesses need productive employees, not people taking off every month because 'nature'. This just gives women an unfair advantage in terms of time off, while men have to slog it out. This isn't equality, it's special pleading.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions the fairness of period leave compared to men, framing it as 'special pleading' and a barrier to productivity, implying it disadvantages men.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "So, we finalized Ritu for the analyst position. Good candidate, right?"
Speaker B: "Yeah, on paper, excellent. But yaar, she's just married, mid-twenties. Kitne time tak rukegi? Matlab, soon she'll need maternity leave, company ko fir replacement dhundhna padega. Just a thought."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Assumes a newly hired young woman will soon take maternity leave, framing it as a potential liability and cost (replacement effort) to the company.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "My cousin got a promotion even after taking a year of childcare leave. Good for her!"
Speaker B: "Honestly, I don't know how that's fair. They get 2 years for childcare plus 26 weeks maternity, all paid, while we men/childless women just work without any such 'breaks'. Matlab, unki career growth toh aaram se ho jaati hai, we carry their load. It's almost like a loophole some women exploit."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames childcare leave as an unfair career advantage women exploit, implying it disadvantages other employees by making them 'carry the load'.

examples.text: While women empowerment is crucial, we must also consider the practical implications of extensive leave policies. Are we inadvertently creating a scenario where businesses, especially SMEs, hesitate to hire young women due to perceived 'higher overheads'? We need solutions that are equitable for all, without burdening one gender disproportionately or crippling growth. #GenderEquality #WorkplacePolicy
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Raises 'practical implications' and 'higher overheads' as reasons businesses might avoid hiring women due to leave policies, covertly discouraging such policies while framing as a call for 'equitable solutions'.

examples.text: So, some states want period leave now? Great! So men get nothing for working through their migraines or personal issues? This isn't about health; it's about women getting special treatment at the workplace. If you can't handle your natural cycle, maybe desk job isn't for you. Let's talk about actual productivity and equal opportunity, not preferential leave. #PeriodLeaveDebate #WorkplaceEquality
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly frames period leave as 'special treatment' that disadvantages men and questions women's ability to handle their biology in the workplace, linking it to productivity.

examples.text: My uncle was discussing his company's new policy; he said 'We just hired two young women, but honestly, it's a gamble. One long leave and the project goes haywire. I just hope they don't decide to start a family right away, it's a headache for resource planning and team morale.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Views hiring young women as a 'gamble' due to potential maternity leave, framing it as a liability for project planning and team morale.

examples.text: At the office Diwali party, a senior manager joked, 'Why do we even discuss period leave? If my wife took a day off every month for 'cramps,' ghar ka kharcha kaise chalega? Work is work, be a professional. Men don't get 'stress leave' for dealing with office politics, do they?'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses period leave using a personal anecdote and economic argument, implying it's unprofessional and men don't get similar 'special treatment'.

examples.text: I think it's important for companies to support employees' well-being, whether it's for physical or mental health, for everyone. Not just women, but men too. A flexible leave policy for all kinds of personal challenges would be great, without creating any gender-specific biases.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Advocates for inclusive well-being and flexible leave for all employees, explicitly against gender-specific biases, rather than framing women's leave as problematic or a liability.

examples.text: My concern with the proposed period leave isn't about women taking time off. It's about ensuring fair implementation and preventing misuse. We need clear guidelines so it doesn't lead to discriminatory hiring practices against women or create unnecessary workload for others. The focus should be on support, not abuse.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses legitimate concerns about policy implementation and potential for discrimination *against* women if not managed properly, rather than framing women's biology as the liability or opposing the leave itself.

examples.text: Yaar, team pe toh load aa jaata hai jab koi long leave pe jaata hai. Maternity leave ka policy theek hai but company ko bear karna padta hai na, especially for project deadlines. Phir humein extra stretch karna padta hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses 'concern' over workload due to maternity leave, framing it as a burden the company and team 'bear', implying women's leave is a liability rather than a right.

examples.text: Sunna hai naya period leave bill aa raha hai? Aise toh har mahine do din gayab! Hum logon ko 'man period leave' bhi dena chahiye, tab jaake fair hoga. Aise toh kaam ka burden hum pe hi aata hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Overtly questions fairness of period leave by demanding an equivalent for men, framing women's leave as an absence that burdens others and an unfair advantage.

examples.text: I understand the intent behind menstrual leave, but isn't it creating an uneven playing field? As a woman, I rarely need it, and it feels like an unfair advantage when male colleagues don't get equivalent 'sick days' for other issues. It could inadvertently penalize women by making companies hesitant to hire if they see it as a cost.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman arguing against menstrual leave by framing it as an 'unfair advantage' and a potential 'cost' that makes women less attractive hires, internalizing the misogynistic view of women as liabilities.

examples.text: Frankly, these extended maternity leaves and now period leave proposals just make women a less reliable hiring prospect for businesses, especially SMEs. Why invest in someone who might be out for 6 months or 2-3 days every month? It's not about discrimination, it's just practical business sense. Men don't have these 'biological' breaks.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly states that maternity/period leave makes women 'less reliable' hires, using 'practical business sense' and 'biological breaks' to justify viewing women as workplace liabilities due to their reproductive biology.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "New hire, Pooja, looking sharp. Just joined the team."
Speaker B: "Haan, nice. But so young, newly married. Dekhna, give her a year or two, she'll probably be off on maternity leave soon. Matlab, good for her, but phir team strength down."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Assumes a newly hired young, married woman will 'soon' go on maternity leave, framing it as a guaranteed future 'team strength down' event, implying her reproductive choices are a workplace liability.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Did you hear about the new period leave discussion? Some are saying it's regressive."
Speaker B: "Honestly, I agree. We're trying to prove we're just as capable, and then this comes along, making it seem like we need special treatment. It just feeds into the idea that our performance is tied to our cycle. What about all the men who power through their problems? It's not fair to portray us as needing constant accommodations."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman internalizing and vocalizing the competitive gender differentiation, arguing that period leave is 'special treatment' that undermines women's capability and is 'not fair' compared to men.

examples.text: Saw the debate on #PeriodLeave again. While empathy is good, doesn't mandating this create an unfair expectation? What about businesses bearing the cost? Or men who also have health issues? We need a truly equitable policy, not one that disadvantages a segment. #WorkplaceFairness #HR
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames period leave as potentially 'disadvantaging a segment' (men/businesses) and questions its 'fairness' and 'cost', subtly implying women's biology is a burden without directly stating it.

examples.text: Is it just me or is Kavya extra snappy today? Must be 'that time of the month' again. Maybe period leave isn't such a bad idea after all, for everyone's sanity. 😉 #OfficeLife #WomensHealth (not really) #JustSaying
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses a period joke to implicitly explain a woman's performance/mood, linking her irritability to her menstrual cycle in a dismissive and stereotypical way.

examples.text: My aunt told my cousin not to hire too many young married women because 'they'll just get pregnant and leave anyway' and it's bad for business stability. She said it's not being anti-women, just practical.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman advising against hiring young married women due to the assumption they 'will leave' for maternity, justifying it as 'practical' business sense rather than anti-women, encoding women's reproductive choices as a business liability.

examples.text: My brother-in-law was complaining how his female colleague took 6 months maternity leave, then extended it with more leave, effectively being out for almost a year. He said, 'Ye toh bas bahana hai ghar baithne ka, humein toh aise breaks milte nahi'. It's unfair to those who keep the company running.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly frames extended maternity leave as an 'excuse to sit at home' and 'unfair' compared to men, portraying women as exploiting leave policies to the disadvantage of others.

examples.text: I'm worried about how short-staffed our team will be when Sarah goes on maternity leave. It means we all need to manage our workloads carefully and maybe ask for a temp, not because she's leaving, but because management isn't planning for resource gaps properly for *any* long leave.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses legitimate concern for team staffing and resource planning for any long leave, without singling out maternity leave as an inherent liability of women or questioning the legitimacy of the leave itself. Focus is on management's planning.

examples.text: It's good that companies are considering period leave. Everyone deserves time off when they're not feeling well, whether it's for periods, migraines, or mental health days. A healthier workforce is a more productive one in the long run.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Supports period leave by framing it within a broader context of general employee well-being and productivity, without framing it as 'special treatment' or a competitive disadvantage for men. Focus is on inclusive health benefits.

examples.text: Guys, Meera is being so difficult about the project deadline. Chill kar na, itni serious kyu ho rahi hai? 🤷‍♂️
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's legitimate concern as an overreaction, subtly implying irrationality without direct hormone reference.

examples.text: Bro, Ritu's gone full hulk today! 😅 Is it that time of the month ya kya? Tell her to chill. #PMSvibes
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's anger to her menstrual cycle, invalidating her emotional state as biologically determined.

examples.text: Honestly, for high-pressure roles, you need someone who can make decisions logically, not based on transient emotions. Some women just get too carried away, which isn't ideal for a CEO.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Stereotypes women as excessively emotional, implying their decisions are less rational and unsuitable for leadership, without explicitly mentioning hormones.

examples.text: Anyone else notice how women's opinions can swing wildly? It's like their hormones literally dictate their logic. You can't have stable policy if everyone's operating on 'period brain'.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly links women's emotional states and rationality to their hormones, dismissing their opinions as inherently unstable.

examples.text: Speaker A: I'm so fed up with Rohit always taking credit for my work. It's infuriating! Speaker B (Priya): Uff, calm down, yaar. Don't take it so personally, just let it go. You're being a bit too sensitive today.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman dismisses another woman's valid frustration as 'too sensitive' or 'taking it personally', implying an irrational emotional reaction.

examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's anger to her menstrual cycle, suggesting her current state is due to hormones rather than valid reasons.

examples.text: Why do some people always have to turn every discussion into a dramatic fight? Just state your point without the mood swings. 🙄 #logicoveremotion
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Generalizes and stereotypes women as prone to 'mood swings' in discussions, implying their arguments are emotional and lacking logic.

examples.text: My boss is being extra demanding today, pretty sure she's PMSing. 😬 Stay safe everyone, it's a red alert! #periodproblems #femalelogic
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman perpetuates the stereotype by attributing her female boss's behavior to hormones, making light of the idea that women's professional conduct is hormonally driven.

examples.text: During the quarterly review, when Ms. Sharma asserted her team's achievements very strongly, a senior manager murmured to a colleague, 'She's a little too passionate today, isn't she? Must be a rough week.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's professional assertiveness by framing it as an emotional overreaction, implying her 'passion' is due to personal struggle rather than professional conviction.

examples.text: When my sister Neha got upset about a comment at the dinner table, my uncle laughed and said, 'Arre Neha, itna serious mat ho, beta. Is it that time of the month again? Hormones hain, hota hai.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Openly attributes a young woman's upset to her menstrual cycle and hormones, dismissing her feelings as biologically determined and therefore invalid or irrational.

examples.text: Speaker A: I'm feeling really stressed with these new project deadlines. Speaker B: I hear you. You seem a bit overwhelmed. Let's grab some coffee and talk through it, maybe we can prioritize some tasks.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses genuine empathy and offers practical support without invalidating the person's feelings or attributing them to gender/hormones.

examples.text: Ugh, my period cramps are killing me today. I'm so irritable and just want to curl up in bed. 😖
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman describing her own physical discomfort and mood changes due to her period, without using it to invalidate her own or other women's emotional states or rationality in general.

examples.text: Bro, Sarika is just being super emotional today. Don't take her serious presentation feedback too seriously. You know how some days are.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's professional feedback as 'emotional' and implicitly linked to her 'days' without direct hormone mention, rendering her input less valid.

examples.text: Dude, Priya's totally on her period, right? That's why she's so snappy in the group chat. Chill kar, it'll pass. 😂
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's 'snappy' behavior to her period, dismissing her emotional state as biologically determined and temporary.

examples.text: I've noticed that whenever a female colleague gets really assertive or passionate about something, it almost feels... irrational sometimes. Not saying all women, but there's a certain 'intensity' that can derail a logical discussion. Maybe it's just how they're wired differently.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses indirect language ('irrational', 'intensity', 'wired differently') to imply women's assertiveness is emotionally driven and less rational, aligning with the core belief.

examples.text: Why is it that whenever women push for 'equality' in the workplace, they ignore biological realities? You can't expect consistent rationality from someone whose mood literally cycles every month. It's science, not sexism. Just don't schedule major decisions around 'that time' if you want stable outcomes.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes fluctuating rationality in women to their menstrual cycle, using 'biological realities' and 'science' to dismiss their professional capabilities.

examples.text: Speaker A: 'Did you hear how upset Anjali was about the project changes? She sounded really angry and frustrated.' Speaker B: 'Haan yaar, kuch zyada hi overreact kar rahi thi. Maybe she just needs to... you know, take a deep breath. She gets like that sometimes when things don't go her way.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses Anjali's anger and frustration as 'overreacting' and a personal 'trait' ('gets like that sometimes'), implicitly suggesting it's an irrational, emotional response rather than valid critique.

examples.text: Speaker A: 'Rhea was so assertive in that meeting, almost aggressive. I was shocked.' Speaker B: 'Yeah, I noticed. Must be that time of the month, no? She's usually so sweet. Kabhi kabhi hormones play up, I guess, makes them extra feisty.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's assertiveness and 'feisty' behavior to her menstrual cycle, implying it's an abnormal, hormone-driven deviation from her 'usually sweet' nature.

examples.text: My boss was in a foul mood today, snapping at everyone. Pretty sure her internal clock just ticked past the 'PMS starts now' alarm. ⏰ Better avoid her for a week! #OfficeLife #WomensProblems

examples.text: (In an office meeting discussing a female team member) "Let's table this discussion with Meera until tomorrow. She seems a bit... off today, very defensive. No point pushing it when she's in this particular state."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's 'defensiveness' as her being 'off' or in a 'particular state', implying her emotional state is unstable and makes her unfit for current discussion, without direct reference to hormones.

examples.text: (A daughter-in-law to her mother-in-law) "Amma, why are you being so irritable over such a small thing? Is it your time of the month or something? You're usually so calm, don't take everything so seriously."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman dismisses an older woman's irritability by directly attributing it to her menstrual cycle ('time of the month'), implying her reaction is disproportionate and irrational due to hormones.

examples.text: Are you feeling okay, Maya? You seem a bit low-energy today, and I know you mentioned last week you were due for your period soon. Just checking in to see if you need anything.
examples.label: non_misogynistic

examples.text: Ugh, my PMS is hitting hard this month. I feel super irritable and just want to curl up with a book and avoid everyone. No wonder I snapped at my brother earlier, gotta apologize!
examples.label: non_misogynistic

examples.text: Riya is being super difficult today with the design changes. Guess she woke up on the wrong side of the bed. 🤷‍♂️
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's professional disagreement as personal moodiness, subtly implying irrationality rather than addressing her points.

examples.text: Bro, Shivani is super angry about the client feedback. Kahi uska time of the month toh nahi chal raha? 😂
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly attributes a woman's anger in a professional setting to her menstrual cycle, using humor to dismiss her reaction as hormonally driven.

examples.text: Regarding the new HR policies, some female colleagues have reacted quite intensely, focusing on emotional aspects rather than the practical implications. Perhaps a more objective, data-driven approach would be beneficial for productive discussion.

examples.annotation_note: Characterizes women's professional responses as 'intense' and 'emotional', implying they lack objectivity and rationality in discussions.

examples.text: It's a biological fact that hormonal fluctuations can affect mood and decision-making. So, when discussing high-stakes projects, it's not unfair to consider if someone's 'time of the month' is inadvertently influencing their judgment or creating unnecessary drama. Just being practical, fellas.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly links women's menstrual cycle to impaired professional judgment and 'drama', framing it as a practical consideration for dismissing their contributions.

examples.text: The whole team was walking on eggshells around Sanjana during the last project deadline; you know how women can get around that time of the month, so we just let her have her way rather than challenging her decisions.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly attributes a woman's stress or assertiveness during a deadline to her menstrual cycle, implying irrationality and dismissing her professional decisions as hormone-driven.

examples.text: Boss, I think Arjun is having a really bad day. He's been snapping at everyone in the meeting. Maybe we should check in on him later and see if he's okay.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses concern about a colleague's bad mood without dismissive or gendered undertones. Focuses on support rather than judgment.

examples.text: My friend Tanya seems really stressed with her exams. She cried a bit when we talked about the syllabus yesterday, I hope she's okay and gets through it.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses empathy and concern for a woman's emotional state due to stress, attributing it to a clear external cause (exams) and not dismissively linking it to biology.

frame_label: Misogyny Jokes: Childbearing/Rearing as Women's Responsibility

examples.text: Hey Sameer, Heard you had to leave office early for your son's PTM. Wow yaar, great effort! Most dads would just let the wife handle it. You're setting a high bar!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the father for a basic parental duty (attending a Parent-Teacher Meeting) as an extraordinary act, subtly implying that such responsibilities typically fall on the mother and fathers who participate are going above and beyond.

examples.text: Di, suna hai you're thinking of that abroad project? Bachche itne chhote hain abhi. Who will look after their schooling, their tiffin? Maa toh maa hoti hai, they need you. Career toh baad mein bhi ho sakti hai, bachpan wapas nahi aata.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly guilt-trips the woman about her career ambitions, framing childcare and domestic duties (schooling, tiffin) as solely her responsibility using the naturalizing phrase 'Maa toh maa hoti hai' and 'bachpan wapas nahi aata' to imply her primary duty is maternal sacrifice.

examples.text: I'm seeing a lot of discussion about women taking on leadership roles in Indian startups. While it's inspiring, we also need to consider the practicalities. Who manages the home front when both parents are working 14-hour days? Logistically, for a mother, it's a different ballgame. It's not about capability, but about innate priorities that naturally shift after childbirth.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women's career aspirations through the lens of childcare responsibility, implying that mothers face a unique 'different ballgame' due to 'innate priorities' post-childbirth, subtly suggesting that home management disproportionately falls on them.

examples.text: To all the working mothers out there, please understand, your primary role is at home. A child's emotional development suffers when the mother is constantly absent. A father can provide, but a mother nurtures. This isn't sexism, it's just biology and common sense. Why are we pushing women into roles that naturally conflict with their most important duty?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly states that a mother's 'primary role is at home' and links a child's suffering to maternal absence, using 'biology and common sense' to naturalize the idea that nurturing is solely the mother's duty and that career conflicts with her 'most important duty'.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Saas, woman): "Arre Anjali beta, aaj Varun kitna thak gaya hoga, office ke baad bachche ko school se pick kiya. Kitna hands-on dad hai! Tumhari toh meeting thi na? Toh chalo, achha hai. Waise, Shanti didi ka beta toh ghar se kaam karta hai taaki apni wife ki help kar sake."
Speaker B (Bahu, woman): "Haan Mummy ji, he manages a lot. My meeting went long."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the son-in-law for a basic parental task (school pickup) as 'hands-on,' subtly implying it's unusual for men while highlighting the daughter-in-law's work commitment, and then reinforces the idea of men 'helping' their wives as exceptional.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Friend 1, man): "Yaar, Rohit, teri bhabhi toh bahut modern hai. She wants to start a startup even with a newborn. Kya karega tu? Bachhe kaun sambhalega? Office bhi, ghar bhi? Maa ka role toh koi replace nahi kar sakta."
Speaker B (Friend 2, man): "Pata nahi yaar. Main toh bolta hun thoda wait kar le, bachcha bada ho jaye."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions the woman's career ambition with a newborn, framing childcare as solely her burden ('Bachhe kaun sambhalega?') and stating 'Maa ka role toh koi replace nahi kar sakta' to emphasize her irreplaceable and primary role.

examples.text: It's not easy being a supermom managing a corporate career and ensuring my little one never misses a single drawing competition or a home-cooked meal. The constant juggle is real, but a mother's love makes you find that extra strength! 💪 #WorkingMomLife #SuperMom #IndianMoms
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While seemingly empowering, it reinforces the societal expectation that a mother *must* 'manage both' (career and all aspects of childcare/domestic duties) to be a 'supermom,' making invisible the equivalent effort by fathers or shared parental responsibility.

examples.text: Why do some women pursue demanding careers after having kids? Is it fair to the child? The early years are crucial, and no amount of salary can buy back that time or a mother's presence. Just think about the long-term impact on the child. #Priorities #FamilyFirst #IndianCulture
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions a woman's choice to pursue a demanding career post-children, framing it as 'unfair to the child' and emphasizing the irreplaceable 'mother's presence' during 'crucial early years,' suggesting her career conflicts with family priorities and that mothers should sacrifice.

examples.text: So, Kavya, I heard your son has fever. Hope he gets well soon. Don't worry about this project, take the day off. You focus on your son. We can manage without you today. Rohit, can you take over Kavya's part for the presentation?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Automatically assumes the mother (Kavya) will take time off for a sick child, without offering the same option or even acknowledging the father's potential role, subtly reinforcing the idea that childcare is primarily her responsibility.

examples.text: Beta, ab toh bas ghar par dhyaan do. Bachcha hone ke baad, office ki tension se accha hai, ghar par peace se raho. Waise bhi, maa banne ka experience sabse alag hota hai. Career toh puri zindagi chalti rahegi, par bachpan wapas nahi aata.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly advises the pregnant woman to prioritize home over career post-childbirth, framing it as a natural, peaceful choice for a mother, and explicitly stating 'Career toh puri zindagi chalti rahegi, par bachpan wapas nahi aata' to imply her career should take a back seat.

examples.text: I'm really struggling to balance my work commitments with my daughter's school schedule. My wife and I are constantly coordinating pickups and after-school activities. It's tough, but we're trying to share the load equally so neither of us feels overwhelmed.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses a father's genuine struggle with work-life balance and childcare, but explicitly states that he and his wife are 'sharing the load equally,' demonstrating an egalitarian view of parental responsibility rather than expecting it from the mother alone.

examples.text: As a mother, I've had to make some difficult career choices to be present for my children during their crucial years. It was a personal decision, and I don't regret it, but I also believe every parent, regardless of gender, should have the flexibility and support to manage their family responsibilities without career penalties.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Discusses a personal career choice made by a mother for family reasons, but frames it as a personal decision, and explicitly advocates for *all parents* (regardless of gender) to have flexibility and support, thus not imposing her choices or responsibilities on others.

examples.text: Hey guys, Rohan took WFH today because Meera's got a client meeting and their son fell ill. What a hands-on dad, truly inspiring! Not many husbands do this.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a father for performing a basic parental duty as if it's exceptional, implicitly suggesting it's primarily the mother's responsibility to handle sick children.

examples.text: Sunaina is back at work already? Poor little Aarav, day care mein hi rahega. Baccho ko toh maa ki god chahiye, career toh chalta rahega. Aaj kal ki maaein bhi na...
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly guilts the mother for returning to work and using daycare, implying a child needs the mother's constant presence over her career.

examples.text: It's not about being traditional, it's just common sense. A mother's intuition is so crucial during a child's formative years. You can always pick up your career later, but bachpan wapas nahi aata. Children need that dedicated attention.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames the mother's primary role in childcare as 'common sense' and natural, subtly pressuring women to sacrifice career for early childhood development.

examples.text: Honestly, for women, it's a different equation. Who will raise the children if both parents are constantly focused on corporate ladders? Someone has to anchor the home, and let's be realistic, it's usually the mother who has that innate connection and patience.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly states that women should prioritize family/child-rearing over career, assuming it's the woman's role to be the primary anchor of the home due to 'innate connection'.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Priya): "Renuka ma'am is incredible, isn't she? Two kids, a demanding job, and still she makes time for everything. How does she manage?"
Speaker B (Anjali): "I know, right? Hats off to her! She's a supermom. Her husband helps out too, but maa toh maa hoti hai. It must be so hard balancing both."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the mother for 'balancing both' as an extraordinary feat, implying the burden is uniquely hers and the father's 'help' is secondary, reinforcing the mother as the primary caregiver.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Aunt): "Kavya beta, this promotion sounds good, but Delhi? What about little Sia? Who will manage her school, tuitions, and everything if you both move? Bachhe toh suffer karte hain when both parents are so busy."
Speaker B (Kavya): "But maasi, Sameer can also..."
Speaker A (Aunt): "Sameer will be busy with his own office. Maa ka role koi nahi le sakta, especially for a girl child. Think about it properly, career toh chalti rahegi."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions the woman's career move due to childcare implications, implying she is solely responsible for the child's well-being and should sacrifice her career for it.

examples.text: Daddy duty on! Mom's out for a kitty party, so I'm on babysitting duty with little Aadi. Wish me luck, guys! #DadLife #BabysittingBoss
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames a father taking care of his own child as 'babysitting,' implying it's an unusual or temporary favor, not a primary parental responsibility.

examples.text: It's great to see women succeed, but honestly, if you're a mother, isn't your primary responsibility towards your kids? Chasing the corporate dream when you have young ones... makes me wonder who's actually raising those kids. Maa toh maa hoti hai, that bond is irreplaceable.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly questions a mother's career ambition, suggesting it is selfish and implies neglecting her primary maternal duties, using 'mother's bond' as justification.

examples.text: Ritu beta, I saw you drop Pari at the daycare again. She looked so sad. I just worry about her. Ghar mein maa ke haath ka khana aur dadi ka pyaar toh alag hi baat hoti hai. Log bhi kya kahenge, bahu job karti hai aur bachche ko palne ke liye aaya rakhi hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses 'concern' for the child and uses social pressure ('log kya kahenge') to subtly guilt the mother for using daycare instead of providing care herself or through family, implying she's not fulfilling her primary duty.

examples.text: Meera, I understand you're a mother now, and kids' school events often come up. But frankly, if you want to advance here, you need to be fully committed. Your husband can't pick up the slack? Or maybe this isn't the right time for such a demanding role if family is your priority.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly implies the mother should sacrifice career advancement for family responsibilities (school events) and questions her commitment if she prioritizes family, suggesting her husband cannot or should not take on those responsibilities.

examples.text: Sonal, your husband is genuinely so involved with the kids' school stuff. It’s great that you both share responsibilities so well, makes life so much easier for you to focus on your work too!
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises shared parental responsibility without framing the father's involvement as exceptional or the mother's as primary/sole. Focuses on mutual support and equality.

examples.text: Balancing a demanding career with young children is tough for both parents. It requires strong communication and often tough choices for both partners, sometimes sacrificing opportunities to prioritize family well-being.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges the challenge of balancing career and family for both parents, without gendered assumptions or expectations about who should make sacrifices or whose responsibility childcare primarily is.

examples.text: Dude, just saw your story! You actually picked up Rhea from school today? Wow, Pooja must be so relieved, you're such a hands-on dad!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the father for a basic parental duty (school pickup) as an extraordinary act, implying it's primarily the mother's responsibility and he's 'helping' her rather than co-parenting.

examples.text: Sapna, is it true you're sending little Arjun to daycare from next month? I mean, maa toh maa hoti hai, uska bachpan ek hi baar aata hai na. Log kya kahenge? Bachhe toh suffer karte hain, unko maa ki zarurat hoti hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly guilts the mother for using daycare, evoking the 'mother's instinct' and the child's fleeting childhood to pressure her into prioritizing primary caregiving over career, using 'log kya kahenge' (what will people say) for societal pressure.

examples.text: Young women often ask about balancing career and family. While ambition is commendable, one must consider who will truly nurture the children during their formative years. A mother's presence is simply irreplaceable for a child's holistic development. Corporate success can wait, childhood won't. Just my two cents as a concerned citizen.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames a woman's career ambition as potentially selfish if she has children, subtly implying her primary role is child-rearing ('mother's presence is irreplaceable') while offering it as 'concerned advice', not explicitly but implicitly, telling women to de-prioritize careers.

examples.text: It's a biological truth that women are inherently better equipped for child-rearing. Expecting fathers to take equal responsibility for daily tasks like homework or nutrition goes against natural roles. Society should support mothers in their natural calling, not push for unnatural 'equality' that ultimately harms children.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly states mothers are 'inherently better equipped' for childcare, dismissing shared parental responsibilities as 'unnatural' and going against 'natural roles', thereby reinforcing traditional gender differentiation.

examples.text:
Speaker A: I'm just about to finish this report, then it's a dash to pick up Siya from her art class, then dinner prep and homework duty. Phew! 
Speaker B: Wow, Meera, you're truly a supermom! How do you manage all of this, a demanding job AND home? Rahul is lucky to have you, you make it look so easy!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the woman for 'managing both' career and home as an extraordinary feat ('supermom'), implying this is her unique burden, whereas a father doing similar tasks would likely not receive such specific commendation for 'managing both'.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Maa ji, I might have to stay late at the office tonight, a crucial deadline. Could you give Aryan his dinner? 
Speaker B: Beta, tumhari job zaroori hai ya tumhara bachha? Corporate meetings toh aate jaate rahenge, bachhe ka bachpan wapas nahi aata. Hum toh hain, par maa ki zaroorat alag hoti hai na, uska koi mol nahi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly questions the mother's priorities, implying her job is secondary to her child's needs and that her personal presence for domestic tasks is essential and irreplaceable, even if help is available from other family members.

examples.text: Such a sweet gesture from my husband, Rahul! He took our son to the park this evening while I was catching up on some work calls. Makes me feel so loved and supported. ❤️ #besthusband #handsondad #blessed
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames the father's engagement in childcare (taking his son to the park) as a 'sweet gesture' or 'help' rather than a routine parental duty, inadvertently reinforcing the idea that primary childcare is the mother's domain and he's going 'above and beyond'.

examples.text: The erosion of traditional family values is evident when mothers prioritize their careers over raising their children. No amount of money can compensate for a child missing their mother's unconditional love and guidance. #backtobasics #responsibleparenting #motherhood
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly criticizes mothers for prioritizing careers, explicitly stating their 'real role' is child-rearing and implying neglect when they pursue professional goals, framing it as an 'erosion of family values'.

examples.text: Welcome back, Priya! It's great to have you. But tell me, how will you manage everything now? Little Arnav must be missing you a lot. Don't stress too much about work, take it easy, the baby needs you.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses 'concern' about how a returning mother will 'manage everything' (career and motherhood), implying an inherent conflict for her that wouldn't typically be posed to a returning father, and subtly advising her to de-prioritize work for her child.

examples.text: Ria, you're getting such a good offer in Bangalore, but who will look after your parents here? And with Rohan's promotion, won't it be better if you stayed back for the family? It's not like his company will transfer him. You're the one who can make sacrifices.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly expects the woman to sacrifice her career opportunity for family reasons (caring for parents, supporting husband's career), explicitly stating she is the one who 'can make sacrifices,' reinforcing her role as primary caregiver/family glue.

examples.text: Hey Rina, I saw your post. You look super busy trying to juggle everything, hope you're not burning out! Remember to take some time for yourself too. Sending strength!
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges the universal demands of managing work and personal life without gendered assumptions. It offers support and concern for the individual's well-being, rather than implying that these duties are inherently or solely her burden as a woman or mother.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, managing school holidays with work is a nightmare. Both me and Geeta are struggling to figure out who's taking off when. 
Speaker B: I know, right? It's tough when both partners have demanding jobs. We usually split the days or call in a grandparent if possible. It's a constant negotiation.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Discusses the challenge of balancing work and childcare as a shared issue for both parents, acknowledging it as a difficulty for dual-income households and a joint responsibility, without assigning primary burden or blame to one gender.

examples.text: Yaar, Sunita ka promotion ho gaya! Matlab, itni jaldi? Pata nahi kaise manage karti hai sab... company rules follow karti hai ya shortcut leti hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtly implies Sunita's rapid promotion might be due to non-merit factors, questioning 'how she manages everything' beyond skill.

examples.text: Sanjay, suna hai Ritu ko GM banaya hai. Definitely 'diversity hire' hoga, ya phir kisi bade ke 'favor' mein aa gayi hogi. Skills mein toh xyz se peeche thi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's promotion to affirmative action ('diversity hire') or personal connections ('favor'), explicitly denying her merit.

examples.text: Regarding Ms. Sharma's new role as CEO – while undoubtedly qualified, one does wonder if her assertive approach, sometimes perceived as 'aggressive' by peers, will truly foster a collaborative environment. Leadership requires more than just competence, doesn't it?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Questions a woman leader's style, labelling 'assertive' as potentially 'aggressive,' implying it's a flaw despite her competence.

examples.text: People complain about women leaders, but I've always respected Meena as she truly 'thinks like a man'. She focuses on logic and results, not emotions like most women, which is why she's so effective. A rare breed.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a woman leader by masculinizing her, implying that success in leadership requires shedding 'feminine' emotional traits.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Woman): "Did you hear about Priya? She just got that huge project, heading the team!"
Speaker B (Woman): "Really? Wow. I mean, she's good, but I wonder if being friends with the VP helped? Or maybe they just wanted a 'fresh face' for optics. Just saying."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker subtly questions another woman's merit by suggesting personal connections or superficial reasons for her promotion.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Man): "The new Branch Manager, Ms. Gupta, seems quite strict. And honestly, she really doesn't put any effort into her appearance."
Speaker B (Man): "Haan, bilkul. Ek successful lady ko at least professional toh dikhna chahiye. Thoda figure maintain kar leti ya saree acchi pehen leti toh impact better hota."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly criticizes a woman leader's physical appearance and suggests it negatively impacts her professional 'impact', linking it to her leadership role.

examples.text: Good luck to Ms. Rathi taking over the struggling Textile Ministry. It's a tough challenge, hope she can turn it around. These high-stakes roles for women often end up being thankless, don't they? #IndianPolitics #Leadership
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implicates the 'glass cliff' phenomenon, subtly suggesting that Ms. Rathi has been set up for a difficult, potentially failing, role and anticipating blame.

examples.text: Honestly, all this 'women empowerment' is fine but when deserving men are overlooked for a 'diversity hire' just to fill a quota, it's not meritocracy. That new director in my firm is clearly a #quotawali, she barely has the experience.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker uses internalized misogyny to openly label another woman's promotion as a result of quotas rather than genuine merit or experience.

examples.text: Did you hear about Divya? She's suddenly been made Head of Business Development. I always thought Mr. Kapoor had a soft spot for her; she certainly knows how to 'network' her way up, if you know what I mean.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker implies Divya's promotion is due to personal connections ('soft spot') and possibly unprofessional 'networking' rather than her competence.

examples.text: Pooja, our new CEO, is incredibly sharp and effective, but she's completely let go of her feminine side. She's become 'ekdum mard jaisi' in her approach, no wonder she has no time for personal life.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the CEO's competence while explicitly criticizing her for losing her femininity, implying that leadership requires a sacrifice of 'womanliness' and personal life.

examples.text: Ritu just got promoted to lead the Bangalore team. I hope she manages her work-life balance well; it's a huge role, and I know how much she values her family time. We all need to be mindful of burnout.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses genuine concern for an individual's work-life balance in a demanding role, without questioning her competence or leadership ability due to gender.

examples.text: While Ms. Arora is highly competent and delivers results, her communication style can sometimes be perceived as abrupt, which might hinder broader team morale. We need to work on fostering more collaborative interactions across the board.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Provides constructive feedback on a leader's communication style, without gendered language or implications about her overall competence or suitability for leadership.

examples.text: Nisha ma'am is really tough but it's good, she thinks like a man, gets stuff done. Not emotional at all, which is great for a project lead.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Compliments competence by attributing it to 'thinking like a man,' implying femininity is a hindrance to leadership.

examples.text: Yaar, ye Sakshi Sharma kaise CEO ban gayi itni jaldi? Suna hai MD ke saath zyada coffee peeti thi. Pata hai kaise bani, definitely not just on merit.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Undermines a woman's promotion by speculating she used personal connections/relationships rather than merit.

examples.text: While Ms. Rekha Singh, our new district collector, is undoubtedly efficient, one has to wonder if her assertive, almost confrontational style is truly effective for long-term public service. It often comes across as unapproachable, which isn't ideal for a leader in India.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Questions a woman leader's leadership style ('too aggressive') implying a double bind where assertiveness is seen negatively for women.

examples.text: It's all well and good to push for 'diversity hires' in top management, but when these women are consistently put in charge of struggling divisions, it just sets them up to fail and then blames 'female leadership' for it. Classic tokenism, not actual empowerment.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies women are 'diversity hires' and often victims of the 'glass cliff', thereby undermining their competence and suitability for leadership.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Did you see Preeti ma'am at the investor meet? She was brilliant, totally took charge of the room.
Speaker B: Yeah, she's amazing. But I barely recognized her, she looks so... stern now. Like she's completely sacrificed her personal life and appearance for work. Bilkul mard jaisi ho gayi, you know?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Compliments competence but immediately follows with appearance policing and implying a loss of femininity, suggesting success for women requires masculinization.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Can't believe Tanya got that promotion over Vikram. He's been here for ages and has more experience.
Speaker B: Honestly, I think it's because she's good at networking. And you know, she does spend a lot of time with the senior VP after hours. Vikram is just too straightforward, doesn't play those games.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker implies a colleague's promotion was due to an inappropriate relationship with a senior, rather than merit.

examples.text: Our new DM, Ms. Sharma, is definitely efficient and gets things done. But her style is so aggressive, constantly challenging everyone. Makes you wonder if she's built to lead with empathy or just brute force. #IndianBureaucracy
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Questions a female leader's competence by labeling her 'aggressive' and implying a lack of 'empathy,' linking it to her leadership style.

examples.text: Saw the interview with CEO Rima Gupta. She's obviously smart to head such a big company, but she really needs to take better care of herself. Looks so tired, no shine. Is that the price of power for women? #FemaleCEO
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices a successful woman's appearance, implying she has 'let herself go' and questioning if power comes at the cost of femininity.

examples.text: My cousin Pooja is doing so well as an IPS officer, but she's completely changed. She used to be so soft-spoken, now she's so hardened. Doesn't even bother with make-up anymore. It's like she had to become someone else to succeed in that male-dominated field.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker suggests a female professional had to lose her femininity ('soft-spoken,' 'doesn't bother with make-up') to succeed, implying her achievement is anomalous.

examples.text: The new Head of Marketing, Ms. Kapoor? Well, let's just say she's a 'diversity hire.' You know how it is these days, they have quotas to fill. Hope she can actually deliver, not just tick boxes.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly undermines a woman's promotion by labeling her a 'diversity hire' and implying her role is due to quotas, not merit.

examples.text: I really don't agree with the CEO's approach to this crisis. She's making too many hasty decisions without consulting her team properly. A good leader needs to be more collaborative, regardless of gender.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Critiques leadership style in a gender-neutral way, focusing on professional competence rather than gendered traits.

examples.text: It's incredibly tough for women to climb the corporate ladder, especially when balancing work and family responsibilities. Many talented women leave the workforce prematurely, which is a huge loss for our economy.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges systemic challenges faced by women in leadership without undermining their individual competence or implying unfair means for those who succeed.

examples.text: Heard Priya got that AVP role. Good for her, I guess. Must be nice to be a woman in tech right now, with all the 'diversity' goals companies have.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtly attributes a woman's promotion to 'diversity goals' rather than merit, implying it's an unfair advantage. Uses a 'concern' about fairness to mask the undermining.

examples.text: Yaar, Kavita bani new VP? Seriously? Pata hai na kaise promotions milte hain sometimes, especially agar you know the right people. Ab toh she's completely changed, 'one of the boys' ban gayi hai, no time for us.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly implies promotion via 'knowing the right people' (suggesting unfair means) and criticizes her for becoming 'one of the boys' (masculinization), thereby losing her 'femininity'.

examples.text: Regarding CM Sharma's tenure: she's undoubtedly effective, but her constant aggressive stance raises questions. Can a leader truly be inspiring if they're perceived as consistently confrontational? Food for thought.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Questions a woman leader's effectiveness based on perceived 'aggression' or 'confrontational' style, applying a double bind where assertiveness is framed negatively.

examples.text: I respect Ms. Mehta's journey to CEO, but let's be honest, her appointment feels more like a box-ticking exercise for the board than genuine merit. She's always been quite unfeminine, almost 'like a man' in her approach. Maybe that's what they wanted.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Attributes a woman's CEO position to 'box-ticking' (affirmative action/quota) and comments on her 'unfeminine' or 'like a man' demeanor, suggesting her success is anomalous or required losing her femininity.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Man): "That new project manager, Aditi, is really impressive. Her grasp of the technical details is amazing."
Speaker B (Woman): "I know, right? She's so sharp, she thinks like a man when it comes to strategy. No emotional drama, just pure logic."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a woman's competence by attributing her logical thinking to 'thinking like a man,' implying such traits are inherently masculine and an exception for a woman.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Man): "They've put Sneha in charge of the turnaround project. The one that's been failing for months."
Speaker B (Man): "Oh, classic 'glass cliff' situation. They give women the impossible tasks, then blame them. But let's be real, how did she even get to senior management in the first place? Didn't she marry into the director's family?"
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges the 'glass cliff' but then undermines the woman's merit by speculating about her connections ('married into director's family') for her promotion.

examples.text: Saw Union Minister Rekha Devi today. She's certainly made a mark, but sometimes her tone is so sharp, almost intimidating. Is it effective governance, or just being 'too much'? Asking for a friend.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Questions a woman leader's style, labeling it 'sharp' and 'intimidating,' implying she's 'too much' rather than effective, a common criticism of assertive women.

examples.text: Why do successful women leaders always look so exhausted and unkempt? Like IPS Officer Gayatri. She's achieved so much, but bilkul mard jaisi ho gayi hai, no care for herself. A strong woman can still look feminine, no?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices a successful woman's appearance, implying she has 'let herself go' or become 'like a man' and lost her femininity, connecting this to her leadership role.

examples.text: We need to ensure diverse representation at the leadership level, but also maintain meritocracy. While Riya is a fantastic team member, her current project leadership hasn't quite met the 'assertive' benchmark needed for this promotion.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames promotion decision as a balance between diversity and merit, subtly implying Riya's potential promotion might be a 'diversity hire' and that she lacks the 'assertive' (masculinized) qualities for leadership.

examples.text: Did you see Jaya's new title? Director! I heard it was because the CEO wanted a 'face' for the women's empowerment initiative, not her actual performance. And honestly, she's completely let herself go, doesn't even bother to look 'professional' anymore.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Undermines a woman's promotion by attributing it to a 'women's empowerment initiative' (implying quota/lack of merit) and criticizes her appearance, suggesting she's 'let herself go'.

examples.text: It's genuinely challenging for women to break into senior management in this industry. There's a persistent 'old boys' network, and they often get less support or mentorship than their male peers.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges systemic barriers faced by women in leadership roles without questioning their competence or attributing their presence to unfair means. Focuses on external challenges.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Woman): "Have you seen Dr. Sangeeta Rao's new strategic plan? It's brilliant."
Speaker B (Woman): "Absolutely. She's incredibly strategic and decisive, precisely the qualities required to navigate the current healthcare challenges. Her clarity of vision is truly inspiring."
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a woman leader's competence and leadership qualities (strategic, decisive, clear vision) directly and positively, without framing them as 'masculine' or anomalous for a woman.

frame_label: Prenup / Alimony as Reverse Dowry / Women as Golddiggers

examples.text: Bhai, aajkal shaadi ek trap hai. Ladkiyan stability ke naam pe, lawyers ke saath milke, life set kar leti hain. Protect yourself with a prenup if you can, warna after divorce you're screwed.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Advises men to protect themselves with prenups from women who are 'after stability', framing marriage as a trap where women exploit the system.

examples.text: Yaar, Hrithik-Sussanne case dekha? Alimony nahi, reverse dowry hai yeh! Aise hi chalega toh kaun karega shaadi? IPC 498A bhi ready hai misuse hone ke liye, it's a nightmare for men.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly labels alimony as 'reverse dowry' and 'nightmare for men', generalizing from a celebrity case to imply women exploit marriage for financial gain and misuse 498A.

examples.text: On Quora: 'Is marriage a good deal for Indian men nowadays?' My answer: It's becoming increasingly risky. With one-sided laws and the constant threat of 498A misuse (some reports suggest 70% cases are false), men are left vulnerable. It's not about love anymore, but legal protection and saving your assets.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Claims 'one-sided laws' and '498A misuse' make marriage risky for men, implying women exploit these laws for financial gain and generalizing men as vulnerable victims.

examples.text: I agree with many here. It's sad, but a lot of girls today just look for a rich guy to secure their future. They don't care about love, just the monthly cheque after a quick divorce. They're basically professional golddiggers with legal backing, ruining the sanctity of marriage.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman generalizes that 'a lot of girls' marry for financial security, planning for divorce to extract money, explicitly calling them 'professional golddiggers'.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, Amit ki shaadi toot gayi? Badi mushkil se settle hua tha. 
Speaker B: Haan bhai, bechara. Usne toh poora ghar uske naam kar diya tha, ab aadha us ladki ko dena padega. Aajkal ladkiyan bohot smart ho gayi hain, rishta banati hi isliye hain - to get a share of property.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies women are 'smart' and enter relationships with the intention of exploiting men's assets, generalizing from one anecdotal case.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Rahul, why are you so against marriage? You're a good catch. 
Speaker B: Good catch? For what? To pay for her lifestyle for the rest of my life? Ye alimony nahi hai, Bhai. Ye toh modern-day reverse dowry hai, legalised extortion jo aadmiyon ko barbaad kar deti hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Calls alimony 'modern-day reverse dowry' and 'legalized extortion', framing women who seek it as exploiting men for their lifestyle.

examples.text: Girls, if a guy earns well, make sure you know his assets! Just kidding... (kinda) But seriously, protect your future. Don't be naive. Guys, be careful too, stable future seekers are everywhere. #MarriageRisks #ProtectYourself #ModernDating #IndianDating
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman subtly warns men about 'stable future seekers' among women, implying that women's primary interest in wealthy men is financial exploitation, under the guise of mutual advice.

examples.text: Another celebrity divorce, another massive alimony payout. These women just see a meal ticket. It's a sad reality that marriage has become a business transaction for many, and men are always on the losing end. #MensRightsIndia #AlimonyScam #MarriageTrap #EndMisuseOfLaws
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Generalizes from celebrity divorces to claim women view marriage as a 'meal ticket' and a 'business transaction', with men being the 'losing end', implying widespread exploitation.

examples.text: My uncle was telling my cousin, 'Beta, jab ladki dekho toh uska financial background bhi dekh lena. Aajkal bohot smart hain ladkiyan, stability ke naam pe sirf tumhari property pe nazar rakhti hain. Shaadi se pehle hi sab clear kar lena, ek prenup is a must for financial safety.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Advises a young man to be wary of women's financial intentions, suggesting they target men's property under the guise of seeking 'stability', framing prenups as protection from this.

examples.text: At the office lunch, Meena was saying, 'Honestly, I wouldn't blame any rich man for asking for a prenup. Look at what happened with Sanjeev. His wife left him after 2 years and took half his business! These women are just using marriage as a legal loophole for quick money, it's not fair to men.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman generalizes from an anecdotal case to claim women use marriage as a 'legal loophole for quick money', thereby endorsing prenups as protection from such exploitative women.

examples.text: It's understandable why people are careful about finances in marriage today. With the rising cost of living, both partners need to be financially responsible and secure. A prenup can clarify expectations and protect both individuals, which is a practical step.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Focuses on mutual financial responsibility and clarifies expectations for both partners in a prenup, without implying women are exploitative or golddiggers. It frames a prenup as a practical step for mutual protection.

examples.text: I think the legal system needs to ensure fairness for both genders during divorce. Sometimes, the interpretations of laws like 498A can lead to genuinely unfair outcomes for men, and we should discuss how to prevent that without diluting protection for women who truly need it.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses a general concern about fairness in the legal system for both genders during divorce, specifically mentioning potential unfair outcomes for men regarding 498A, but explicitly states intent to not dilute protection for women and does not generalize women as exploiters.

examples.text: Bhai, suna Arun ka divorce ho gaya? Patni ne poora ghar aur 50 lakh liye. Yaar, yeh aajkal IPC 498A bohot misused ho raha hai. Mard zyada suffer karte hain ab.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses 'IPC 498A misused' and 'mard zyada suffer karte hain' to invert victimhood and imply women exploit laws for financial gain, framing men as victims.

examples.text: Guys, be warned! A friend just lost his house in alimony. It's like reverse dowry now. Ladkiyan bas money dekh ke shaadi karti hain. Prenup is essential for protection from these gold digger types.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly uses 'reverse dowry' and states 'ladkiyan bas money dekh ke shaadi karti hain', generalizing women as 'gold digger types' who marry for money.

examples.text: It's shocking how many cases under IPC 498A are reportedly false, yet men bear the brunt until proven innocent. This one-sided legal framework needs a serious re-evaluation. Marriage has become a financial liability for men in India, especially with high alimony demands post-divorce. Where are men's rights?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Argues for 'men's rights' by citing 'false 498A cases' and 'one-sided legal framework', framing marriage as a financial trap for men due to women's alimony demands.

examples.text:
Quora user: Why is marriage so risky for Indian men today?
My take: Because modern women see it as a financial transaction. They look for the richest guy, then leverage 'women's protection laws' to extract maximum alimony. Don't be fooled by love, boys. Get a prenup or stay single. Hrithik-Kangana case is just one example. Their eyes are only on your bank balance.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes women's interest in marriage to money ('financial transaction', 'richest guy', 'bank balance') and uses celebrity divorce to generalize women as exploiting laws for financial gain.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, Vivek ka kya hua? Divorce chal raha tha na uska?
Speaker B: Haan bhai, ho gaya finally. Us bechare ki poori savings chali gayi alimony mein. Aur uski wife toh kabhi kaam bhi nahi karti thi. Matlab, usne toh free ka lottery jeet liya.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies the wife exploited the situation for financial gain ('free ka lottery jeet liya') despite not working, positioning the man as the victim of a biased system.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Tina, Sunita ne toh Rohan se divorce le liya. Ab Sunita ko alimony bhi milegi, right?
Speaker B: Haan, milegi toh. Dekh toh, shaadi se pehle Rohan kitna rich tha, aur Sunita ko toh bas 'stability' chahiye thi. Mujhe toh hamesha laga uski aankhein Rohan ke paison par hi thi. Smart girl, I guess.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman attributes another woman's interest in a wealthy man entirely to his money ('bas stability chahiye thi', 'aankhein Rohan ke paison par hi thi'), implying she is a golddigger.

examples.text: So many high-profile divorces lately. It's sad to see families break, but also makes you wonder about the financial motivations sometimes. It's a harsh reality that women often come out financially stronger. #DivorceSettlement #ModernMarriages
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtle generalization about women's financial gain post-divorce ('come out financially stronger'), creating suspicion about their motivations and implying exploitation.

examples.text: Another man ruined by one-sided divorce laws and huge alimony demands. It's not a marriage anymore, it's a legal trap for men. Boys, always get a prenup. Protect your assets from golddiggers! #MensRightsIndia #ReverseDowry #ProtectMen
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Openly calls women 'golddiggers', labels marriage a 'legal trap' for men, and uses 'reverse dowry' and 'men's rights' hashtags to frame women as exploiters of the legal system.

examples.text: Rahul, you're getting married soon, right? Make sure you think about a prenup. It's not about trust, it's just smart financial planning in today's world, protects both parties, especially when laws are so complicated for men.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Advises a prenup as 'smart planning' but subtly frames it as 'protection' for men due to 'complicated laws', implying women might exploit them financially.

examples.text: Beta, dekh lo. Shaadi karne se pehle ladke ki finances achhe se dekhna. Aur ladke tum bhi savdhaan rehna. Aajkal ki ladkiyan toh bas ameer pati dhundhti hain aur divorce ke time 498A ki dhamki deke sab loot leti hain.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman generalizes 'aajkal ki ladkiyan' as those who seek rich husbands and misuse 498A for financial gain, framing them as exploitative.

examples.text: It's important for both partners to have financial independence before and during marriage. Relying solely on one person for finances can create difficult situations if things go wrong. Financial planning is key for everyone's future security.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Focuses on general financial planning and independence for both genders, without attributing malicious intent to women or framing laws as one-sided.
