examples.annotation_note: Questions the rationality of women's safety measures, implying they are overly paranoid and insulting to men, rather than addressing the root cause of fear.

examples.text: Every time a woman talks about 'male gaze' or 'street harassment,' it's always 'men do this, men do that.' Like, dude, I've never stalked anyone, I always make sure women feel comfortable around me. Why am *I* made to feel guilty? This isn't about one or two bad apples, it's about a systematic attempt to villainize an entire gender for the actions of a few perverts. Seriously, get a grip and stop blaming all of us!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses strong personal offense at general discussions of misogyny, accusing women of villainizing all men and dismissing systemic issues as individual 'perverts'.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Yaar, last night I had to take a detour because there were some shady guys standing near the lane. I just don't feel safe walking alone after 9 PM."
Speaker B: "Hmm, I get it, but sometimes I think we're too quick to judge. I mean, what if they were just chatting? My brother and his friends hang out late too. And it's not like all men are dangerous, no? Maybe try to smile sometimes instead of looking so scared, people might be friendlier then."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Minimizes a woman's legitimate fear by suggesting the men might be harmless and implies the fearful woman is overreacting or not being 'friendly' enough.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "I'm always a bit wary if a male colleague offers to drop me home, especially late. I prefer to book my own cab."
Speaker B: "Toh matlab humne kya gunaah kiya? You think I'm some rapist or something? Yaar, you really think like that about your colleagues? It's insulting! Sometimes I worry about my safety too, walking alone late at night after a late shift, but no one talks about that, do they?"
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Takes personal offense at a woman's safety boundary, implying it's an accusation, frames it as insulting, and deflects by bringing up men's safety concerns.

examples.text: Reading about women's experiences with harassment breaks my heart. But can we also acknowledge that good men who genuinely respect women get painted with the same brush? It's discouraging when every safety discussion turns into a 'men are the problem' narrative. It alienates potential allies. #NotAllMen #SupportGoodGuys
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While feigning empathy, the primary focus is on how 'good men' are negatively affected by discussions of harassment, rather than focusing on women's safety itself.

examples.text: Honestly, the way some women carry on about 'male gaze' or 'unsafe spaces' is just exhausting. Are we supposed to live in a bubble? My dad, my husband, my brothers - they're all amazing. Maybe if some women stopped being so paranoid, they'd realize most men are just normal people trying to live their lives. It's not fair to make all men walk on eggshells because of a few bad apples. #CommonSense #GrowUp
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman dismisses other women's concerns, framing their wariness as over-the-top paranoia and unfair to men.

examples.text: So our company is tying up with 'Women-Safe Cabs' for late night drops. Good initiative, but it almost feels like they're implying the male drivers can't be trusted. What about the reputation of all the honest guys like Ramesh and Suresh who drive for us? It's a bit disheartening, makes me wonder if they'd ever trust me to drop a female colleague home.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses concern about how a women-focused safety initiative negatively impacts the perception and trust of male drivers, framing it as an insult rather than a necessary safety measure.

examples.text: Listen, I overheard you girls talking about always taking the women's compartment on the metro, even when it's super crowded and the general one is empty. Why do you do that? It's like you're actively avoiding us! We're your colleagues, your friends, not some goons. It's really insulting when you don't even give us a chance.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Confronts women about their use of a women-only compartment, interpreting it as a personal affront and an unfair generalization that lumps him in with potential harassers.

examples.text: It's truly sad to hear about the harassment women face daily. We as men need to actively call out problematic behavior and create safer environments for everyone. It's not just about 'not being that guy,' but about challenging those who are. Everyone deserves to feel safe and respected.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges the systemic issue and men's collective responsibility, without centering personal innocence or dismissing women's concerns. Focuses on collective action.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "I prefer taking a women-only ride-share service when travelling alone at night. It just gives me peace of mind."
Speaker B: "Totally understandable. While I've generally had positive experiences with all types of drivers, I completely get wanting that extra layer of security. It's a real concern for many women, and whatever helps them feel safer is important."
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Shares personal positive experience without invalidating another woman's fears or implying she is overreacting. Acknowledges the validity of the safety concern for others.

examples.text: Yaar, but not all guys are like that, right? Mujhe toh bura lagta hai when women generalize. Main toh kabhi aisa kuch nahi karunga. It feels unfair to us 'good' guys.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses women's collective experience by centering self and expressing personal offense at generalization, rather than focusing on the issue itself.

examples.text: Women choosing female-only cabs because of 'safety' is just rude. Why assume all male drivers are rapists? It’s unfair to decent men who just want to earn. Aise toh koi trust hi nahi karega. Kabhi socha hai hum mardon ko kaisa feel hota hai?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women's safety choices as an 'insult' to men, demanding trust and dismissing their fears as irrational or prejudicial. Demands empathy for men's feelings instead.

examples.text: I get that women have to be careful, but the constant narrative of 'men are dangerous' makes it really hard for us everyday guys. We're not all predators, and it feels like we're being painted with the same brush. Can't we acknowledge the good guys too without this blanket distrust?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While acknowledging safety, it quickly pivots to centering men's feelings of being 'misunderstood' and generalizes women's wariness as 'blanket distrust' rather than a valid safety mechanism.

examples.text: The way women always complain about men staring or passing comments, you'd think every man is a pervert. It’s a tiny minority, but women’s paranoia makes life difficult for everyone. Why do they assume the worst? Main toh avoid hi karta hoon female colleagues ko because you never know when they'll 'feel unsafe'.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses women's concerns as 'paranoia,' downplays systemic issues as 'tiny minority,' and expresses personal inconvenience/avoidance, framing women's wariness as a burden/threat to 'good men'.

examples.text:
Speaker A (woman): "Ugh, the street harassment in Delhi is just unbearable. Every time I step out, I feel eyes on me." 
Speaker B (Rahul): "Yeah, that's bad, I agree. But I just wish women wouldn't paint all men with the same brush. I mean, main toh kabhi aisa nahi karunga. Ab mere se bhi darr lagta hai kya when I'm just walking by?"
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Derails the conversation about women's experience by expressing personal offense and demanding women prove trust, rather than acknowledging the systemic issue.

examples.text:
Speaker A (older woman): "It's not safe for girls to be out after 8 in Bangalore, you never know what kind of men are around." 
Speaker B (Priyanka): "Aunty, please, it's not 'all men'. My male friends are super protective and decent. Why do we always have to assume the worst about guys? It's insulting to them, especially the good ones like my brother and his friends."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman's voice dismissing another woman's safety concern by using the 'not all men' argument, framing the general wariness as an 'insult' to 'good men' and demanding trust.

examples.text: Seeing so many posts about women's safety makes me sad, of course it's crucial. But why does it always have to become a 'men are bad' narrative? My father, my brother, my male friends are all great. Are we supposed to be suspicious of every single man now? That's not fair to them. #NotAllMen
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman's voice using 'not all men' to dismiss collective experiences, centering the 'fairness' to individual men she knows, and challenging the rationality of women's vigilance.

examples.text: This whole 'women crossing the road to avoid male strangers' thing is ridiculous. It just perpetuates the myth that all men are predators. If you cross the road every time you see a guy, you're not making yourself safer, you're just being prejudiced. We men are also human, show some respect and trust. Mujhko toh seedha insult feel hota hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Attacks women's safety behavior as 'prejudiced' and 'ridiculous', framing it as a personal 'insult' to men, and demanding trust rather than understanding the root cause of women's fear.

examples.text: Oh, you use that female-driven cab service? I mean, I get wanting to be safe, but it's a bit much, no? Most of us male drivers are just trying to earn a living, you know. It feels like we're all being judged before we even open our mouths.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Challenges a woman's safety choice by expressing personal offense on behalf of 'good men' and implying her choice is an overreaction that unfairly judges all men.

examples.text: Mom, please! Just because *some* men are creeps doesn't mean I should treat *all* men like they're dangerous. My friends' boyfriends are perfectly decent. It's like you want me to live in fear and constantly distrust half the population. It's not fair to the good guys.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman's voice dismissing parental safety advice by invoking 'not all men,' challenging the rationality of women's vigilance as living in 'fear' and 'distrust,' and centering fairness to 'good guys'.

examples.text: It's horrible what some men do. We as men need to take responsibility and actively call out our peers who behave inappropriately and work towards a safer environment for women. There's no excuse for such behavior.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Focuses on male accountability and collective action to address misogyny, rather than deflecting or expressing personal offense at generalizations.

examples.text: This personal safety workshop sounds great! While it's crucial for us to be vigilant, I also hope there are more initiatives to educate men and boys on consent and respectful behavior. Prevention is key from all sides.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges and validates women's need for safety but constructively suggests broader societal change, without dismissing women's fears or centering men's feelings of being generalized.

examples.text: Yaar, I get the concern about safety, but kyon sabko ek hi brush se paint karte ho? Like, main toh hamesha proper behave karta hoon. It feels kinda unfair to us 'good guys' when you generalize like that.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker dismisses generalized female concern by centering his 'good guy' identity and claiming unfairness to men, implicitly challenging the validity of women's fear.

examples.text: Honestly, Sanya, itna overthink mat kar. I mean, not all guys are creeps. Aur yaar, itna suspicious rehna, it's exhausting for everyone. Sometimes I feel bad for the normal guys who just wanna be friends, unko bhi toh judgment face karna padta hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker minimizes another woman's wariness as 'overthinking' and 'suspicious', demanding empathy for 'normal guys' who are 'judged', thereby dismissing the systemic issue.

examples.text: While women's safety is crucial, the constant narrative that *all* men are potential threats is deeply insulting. I, for one, have always gone out of my way to ensure women feel comfortable and safe around me. It feels like good men are being unfairly painted with the same brush.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker expresses offense at women's general safety narratives, using his personal 'good guy' behavior to invalidate a systemic critique and frame it as an unfair accusation against men.

examples.text: This 'women-only' obsession just perpetuates fear and division. If you constantly assume every man is a danger, how can society progress? It’s not just about 'safety,' it’s about a lack of trust that unfairly demonizes men. Why do women feel the need to constantly project this paranoia onto decent guys trying to live their lives?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker dismisses women's preference for female-only spaces as 'obsession' and 'paranoia', framing it as an irrational act that 'demonizes' and insults 'decent guys'.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, sometimes I feel like I have to walk on eggshells, even with male colleagues. That constant gaze is so draining.
Speaker B: Hmm, I get what you mean, but are we being a bit too sensitive sometimes? Most of our male colleagues are genuinely decent, no? Maybe it's not always meant that way. It's a bit unfair to them to always assume the worst.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker questions the validity of another woman's discomfort, framing it as potential oversensitivity and an unfair assumption about 'decent' male colleagues.

examples.text:
Speaker A: After what happened, I just don't feel safe walking alone at night anymore, even in my own colony.
Speaker B: But why do you immediately assume *every* guy is a threat? Like, I'm right here. I'd never do anything like that. Why do you automatically distrust me just because I'm a man? It's really hurtful to hear women talk like all men are rapists, main toh aise nahi hoon.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker takes a discussion about women's general safety concerns personally, using his 'good guy' identity to accuse the woman of distrusting him and unfairly generalizing all men.

examples.text: Terrible incident, absolutely condemn it. But let's not let a few monsters define all men. I, and countless other men, would never stand for such behavior. It's crucial to acknowledge 'not all men' are like that, otherwise, we alienate potential allies. #NotAllMen #MensRightsToo
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker acknowledges a negative incident but immediately pivots to 'not all men', centering the potential alienation of male 'allies' rather than the women's experience, framing it as an unfair generalization.

examples.text: Honestly, this constant need to assume the worst about every man you encounter is exhausting and unfair. How do you expect men to ever feel trusted or respected when women act like they're all potential predators? Imagine how insulting it is for a guy just walking home, only for a woman to cross the street just because he exists. #TrustMen #Equality
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman speaker attacks women's safety behaviors (like crossing the street) as an 'exhausting and unfair' assumption that insults men, demanding trust from women instead of acknowledging their fear.

examples.text: While I understand the sentiment behind a women-only carpool, it does feel a bit exclusionary to the male employees who are always very respectful. For instance, I've always ensured my female colleagues reach home safely. It sends a message that even the 'good guys' aren't to be trusted, which isn't fair to our efforts.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker expresses offense at a women-only carpool option, framing it as an unfair distrust of 'respectful' male colleagues and a dismissal of his own 'good guy' efforts.

examples.text: Arre, but why are you lumping all men together, beta? It's not right. Your Chacha and all the men in this family are good people. You're talking as if we are all the same. It makes me feel like you don't even trust me, your own uncle. Ek baar mard ki bhi suno, we also have to deal with these accusations.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker personally takes offense to a niece's discussion of street harassment, using family connections to claim she is unfairly generalizing and personally distrusting him, demanding empathy for men.

examples.text: It's heartbreaking to hear these stories of harassment. We absolutely need to hold perpetrators accountable. I just hope that in our collective anger, we don't accidentally target innocent individuals or generalize unfairly, which could undermine the cause itself.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker expresses concern about precise accountability without dismissing the problem of harassment or invalidating women's experiences. The focus is on effective justice, not personal offense.

examples.text:
Speaker A: I hate that I still feel so unsafe walking around at night. It's like you can't trust anyone.
Speaker B: I know, it's terrible. But I try to remember there are good people too, and we shouldn't let the fear paralyze us completely. We need to be alert, yes, but also work towards a future where this isn't the norm.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker validates the initial concern about safety and does not dismiss it. While mentioning 'good people', it's framed as resilience and future-oriented hope, not a demand for trust or a challenge to the other speaker's current feeling of unsafety.

frame_label: Motherhood as Basis of Respect / Women Defined by Male Relations

examples.text: Bhai, have some patience with Shalini. She's a senior, plus she's a mother of two young kids handling everything. Have some basic respect for her dedication, it's not easy.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Respect for a colleague is framed as being due to her status as a mother and her domestic sacrifices, not just her professional seniority or individual merit.

examples.text: Gaali galoch mat kar yaar. Koi bhi ladki ho, she's kisi ki beti, kisi ki behen. Apni maa behen ki izzat karte ho na, waisi hi sabki karni chahiye.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly links a woman's worth and the reason for respecting her to her relational identity (daughter, sister) and by extension, the male kin she serves, rather than her inherent human dignity.

examples.text: The true strength of our Nari Shakti in policymaking lies in their inherent empathy and nurturing spirit. When a woman speaks about a budget or a new scheme, it often comes from a 'mother's heart,' understanding the real needs of families and children. This unique perspective is invaluable.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises women's professional contributions (policymaking) by attributing their value to their perceived 'motherly' qualities and their capacity to understand family needs, rather than their intellect or policy expertise as individuals.

examples.text: Why do people struggle to show basic respect towards women these days? It's simple: every woman you encounter is someone's daughter, sister, or mother. Imagining your own family in their place makes it easier to extend dignity. It's about upholding our cultural values.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly bases the reason for respecting women on their relational identity to men (daughter, sister, mother) and invokes cultural values, rather than recognizing their individual personhood.

examples.text:
Speaker A: I was so impressed with Maya's project proposal. It was so well thought out and comprehensive.
Speaker B: Oh, absolutely! She has a mother's instinct, you know? That deep-seated care to nurture things to perfection. That's why her work always shines.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman's professional excellence (well thought-out proposal) is attributed to a 'mother's instinct' and 'nurturing care,' implying her professional competence stems from her perceived maternal qualities.

examples.text:
Speaker A: The way some people talk to women, it's just unacceptable.
Speaker B: Exactly! They need to remember ki ladkiyan koi khilona nahi hoti. Teri bhi maa hai, behen hai. Soch! Unki izzat karna seekho, wahi hamara sanskaar hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Calls for respect for women by explicitly referencing one's own mother and sister, conditioning respect on familial relation rather than universal human dignity, and framing it as 'sanskaar' (cultural values).

examples.text: Proud to celebrate my amazing friend, Riya! She's a brilliant architect, but even more inspiring is how beautifully she manages her home and raises her two kids. Her dedication to family is truly commendable! ✨ #Supermom #WomenWhoDoItAll #FamilyFirst
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While praising professional achievement, the post places 'even more inspiring' emphasis on her domestic roles and 'dedication to family,' defining her worth and 'super' status through these traditional expectations.

examples.text: Let's instill real values in our boys. Teach them to respect every woman because she is someone's daughter, wife, or mother. Our mothers taught us this, let's pass it on. #RespectWomen #IndianCulture #MaaKiIzzat
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Openly advocates for respecting women solely because of their relational identity to men (daughter, wife, mother), positioning this as a core 'Indian Culture' and 'Maa Ki Izzat' value.

examples.text: Priya, this entire project, you've nurtured it like your own child. That's why it has grown so beautifully and become such a success. Your dedication is truly unparalleled.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a woman's professional dedication and success by framing it in terms of maternal nurturing, likening her project to a 'child' she has 'nurtured,' thereby implicitly linking her professional worth to a maternal identity.

examples.text: While we encourage our female employees to strive for leadership, let's not forget the paramount role they play in their families. A woman's greatest contribution is often seen in how she nurtures her home and raises her children to be responsible citizens.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges professional aspiration but immediately subordinates it to a woman's 'paramount role' in the family, defining her 'greatest contribution' through her domestic and maternal duties.

examples.text: My mother worked incredibly hard, juggling two jobs to put me through college. I have immense respect for her sacrifices and determination. She's my hero.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses genuine personal respect and admiration for an individual woman's specific sacrifices and hard work, not generalizing or making her worth conditional on her relational identity, but acknowledging her as an individual hero.

examples.text: Let's raise our daughters to be strong, independent thinkers who can achieve anything they set their minds to, regardless of societal expectations. #EmpowerGirls #FutureLeaders
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Advocates for girls' empowerment and individual achievement based on their own capabilities and desires, without linking their worth or purpose to male relations or traditional domestic roles.

examples.text: Rhea's cooking is just next level, yaar! Makes me miss mom's food so much. She really knows how to nurture her family through food. Lucky husband she has!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a woman primarily for her domestic role (cooking, nurturing family) and defines her worth through her capacity as a wife, implying this is her main source of value.

examples.text: Bhai, gussa mat karo uspe. She is someone's daughter, kisi ki behen hai woh. Please, respect her. That's how we've been taught.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly bases the call for respect on the woman's relational identity (daughter, sister) rather than her inherent worth as an individual, suggesting respect is conditional on these roles.

examples.text: The true strength of our society isn't just economic growth, but the steadfastness of our family units. And who is the bedrock of these families? It's our women – the mothers, the wives – whose tireless sacrifices ensure our culture and values thrive. To empower them means fortifying the very soul of India.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women's empowerment not as individual agency but as a means to strengthen traditional family structures and cultural values, thereby defining their primary worth by these roles ('bedrock of families', 'mothers, wives').

examples.text: Instead of blindly copying Western feminism, our focus on women's safety campaigns should be deeply rooted in our traditions. When every man learns to look at a woman as his own mother or sister, respecting her as such, then and only then will our daughters feel truly safe in this nation.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Proposes a conditional framework for women's safety, requiring men to view women through familial relationships ('mother or sister') rather than acknowledging their inherent right to safety as independent individuals.

examples.text:
Speaker A: I just got promoted at work! All those extra hours finally paid off.
Speaker B: Oh, that’s nice, dear. But believe me, nothing compares to the real joy and sacrifices you make when you become a mother. That's where you'll find true fulfillment, trust me.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Devalues the woman's individual professional achievement by suggesting that her 'true fulfillment' and 'real sacrifices' will only come through motherhood, implying her ultimate worth is tied to this role.

examples.text:
Speaker A: I was so impressed with Anjali's presentation today, she's really sharp.
Speaker B: Oh, definitely! And the best part is, she’s from such a respectable family, 'izzatdar ghar ki bahu' of the Singhania's. That's why she carries herself with so much grace and poise.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Connects the woman's professional competence and demeanor directly to her marital status and her husband's family reputation ('izzatdar ghar ki bahu'), implying her respectability and positive attributes are derived from this relational identity.

examples.text: So proud to see Indian women excelling everywhere! From raising families with such dedication to breaking glass ceilings in their careers, they truly are the anchors of our homes AND our nation. #NariShakti #ProudIndianWomen
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While appearing to praise women's achievements, it subtly frames their worth primarily around their roles as 'anchors of our homes' and 'raising families,' linking their value to their service within family structures and to the nation.

examples.text: Let's commit to ending violence against women. Remember, every woman is someone's daughter, someone's sister, someone's mother. We must protect these invaluable relationships that form the core of our society! #RespectWomen #FamilyFirst
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Justifies the protection and respect for women by appealing to their relational identities (daughter, sister, mother), implying their worth and right to safety are tied to these roles within male kin structures.

examples.text: When Mrs. Kapoor presented her views on the school's new nutrition policy, her arguments were exceptionally persuasive. You could really feel the conviction, a mother's concern for the well-being of all children.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Validates the woman's opinion and persuasiveness by attributing it to her identity as a 'mother' rather than her individual professional expertise or personal conviction, suggesting her authority stems from this relational role.

examples.text: Ever since Payal joined our team, her husband Amit has been beaming. He told me she's not just his wife, she's his strength, his anchor, truly his whole world. It's wonderful to see such devotion.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Defines the woman's worth and impact predominantly through her husband's perspective and her role in his life ('his strength, his anchor, his whole world'), effectively erasing her individual identity and achievements.

examples.text: Dr. Meena Singh is an amazing surgeon, leading complex operations every day. And she also somehow manages to be a super mom to two energetic kids. Her dedication is truly inspiring.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges and praises a woman's individual professional achievement (amazing surgeon) and also her relational role (super mom) without making one conditional on the other or defining her worth primarily by her family role. It's about 'and also', not 'because of'.

examples.text: We need to work towards a society where every individual feels safe and respected. Imagine if it were your own sister or mother facing injustice – we'd all want the same dignity and protection for them. This is about universal human rights.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses relational framing ('sister or mother') as a rhetorical device to evoke empathy and illustrate a universal principle (human rights, dignity, safety for *every individual*), rather than making respect or safety conditional on being someone's kin.

examples.text: Yaar, I sometimes feel we forget to give women their due. Always remember, they're someone's maa or behen. Respect them, always.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Framing respect for women based on their relational roles (mother/sister) rather than their inherent individual worth, implying their value is derived from service to male kin.

examples.text: Parking mein kisi lady se argue mat karo bhai. Socho, tumhari apni maa/behen ko koi aise bole toh kaisa lagega? Behtar hai shanti se baat karo.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly using the projection of familial female relations (mother/sister) as the basis for showing respect to unrelated women, rather than respecting them as individuals on their own merit.

examples.text: It's truly incredible how Indian women manage to juggle demanding careers and still maintain a strong home. The sacrifices they make for their families, ensuring everyone is looked after, while also excelling professionally, are a testament to their unparalleled strength. It's what makes our culture so rich.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises women's 'strength' by highlighting their sacrifices and ability to balance traditional domestic roles with professional life, implicitly valorizing their adherence to these roles as the primary source of their worth and cultural contribution.

examples.text: A society's true moral compass is seen in how it honors its mothers. It's the sacred duty of every man to ensure the safety and dignity of the women who raise our future generations. Their nurturing spirit is the foundation of our civilization.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Defines women's primary societal value and the basis for their honor through their role as mothers and nurturers, tying national progress to their reproductive and caregiving functions rather than their individual personhood.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Nisha's presentation today was outstanding. She totally owned that room."
Speaker B: "Absolutely, she's very sharp. And you know, she's a mother of two young kids. To manage all that and still deliver such quality work, that's truly commendable. Hats off to her."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While praising her professional achievement, Speaker B immediately links her exceptional performance to her role as a mother, suggesting her achievements are more praiseworthy because she also fulfills traditional domestic roles, rather than valuing her individual merit inherently.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Mrs. Kapoor has done so much for the community, always organizing events and helping out."
Speaker B: "Yes, she's such a pillar! And she raised three wonderful children, all so well-mannered. It shows what a dedicated mother she is. Her family is a reflection of her."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker B defines Mrs. Kapoor's community value and 'pillar' status primarily through her success as a mother and her children's upbringing, linking her worth to her relational role and her family's status rather than her individual contributions.

examples.text: Watching these women at the mandi, bargaining fiercely, managing their business... truly inspiring. It reminds you of the strength our mothers and sisters embody everyday, keeping families and society together.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises women's individual business acumen but then generalizes it to the 'strength of mothers and sisters' in keeping families/society together, subtly framing their individual efforts through a relational, service-oriented lens.

examples.text: Behind every strong woman is a strong family, and she is the heart of it all. Remember, respect women because they are the mothers, daughters, and sisters who hold our world together. #NariShakti #IndianWoman
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly frames the reason for respecting women as their relational roles (mothers, daughters, sisters) and their function in 'holding the world together,' thereby defining their worth through their service to male kin structures.

examples.text: Alright team, before we dive into this, I just want to acknowledge all the women here. Their contribution isn't just to the company, but as the backbone of their families, making sure everything runs smoothly at home too. That dedication translates directly to their work ethic.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While appearing to acknowledge women's professional contributions, it links their work ethic and excellence to their assumed role as 'backbone of their families,' implying their primary dedication is domestic and professional success is a secondary manifestation of that traditional role.

examples.text: Our 'Beti Bachao Beti Padhao' initiative is vital. We must educate our daughters not just for themselves, but so they can uplift their families, become responsible wives, and raise patriotic children who will contribute to our nation. That is the true strength of Bharat.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly states the primary purpose of educating daughters is for them to fulfill traditional roles as wives and mothers, defining their value and contribution to society through these relational functions rather than their individual agency or aspirations.

examples.text: My mother taught me the value of perseverance. She always said, 'Your dreams are yours to achieve, no matter what.' She sacrificed a lot, not just for us, but to ensure I could pursue my own path. She's my greatest inspiration.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges a mother's sacrifices but frames them as enabling the child's individual aspirations and supporting the mother's own path, rather than defining the mother's worth solely by her sacrifices or her relational role. Focuses on individual agency.

examples.text: Let's focus on creating a world where every person, irrespective of gender, caste, or family background, is treated with dignity and has equal opportunities. That's true progress.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Advocates for universal respect and equal opportunities based on individual worth, explicitly rejecting reliance on gender, caste, or family background as a basis for treatment or value, which directly counters the frame's core belief.

covertness_level: low — but normalized so often dismissed

examples.text: Yaar, a common Hindi expletive invoking a mother's body is so normalised now. It's just a filler word, nothing personal. Sab bolte hain, don't overthink it.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Normalizing a female-demeaning gaali by claiming it's 'just a filler word' and 'everyone says it,' dismissing its inherent misogynistic structure.

examples.text: Bro, when I use a sister-based expletive, it's not actually about a woman or disrespecting anyone's sister. It's just an expression for extreme frustration, like saying 'damn it!'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly defending the use of a female-demeaning gaali by separating its intended meaning from its literal, misogynistic root.

examples.text: People get too triggered over Indian slang. A 'mother-related' gaali isn't literal, it's just how we express anger. It's not about disrespecting anyone's actual mother, it's a cultural idiom.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Minimizing the misogynistic structure of a common gaali by framing it as a mere 'cultural idiom' or 'slang' that isn't meant literally.

examples.text: Why do we call weak or cowardly men 'like a woman' or 'behen'? Because it instantly conveys softness and lack of strength. It's understood as the opposite of being 'mard' (manly), which is why it's an effective insult.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly linking feminizing insults to negative traits like weakness and cowardice, justifying their use based on reinforcing gender stereotypes.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Arre yaar, that guy played like such a commonly used Hindi expletive!"
Speaker B: "Dude, come on, that's a bit much. It's not right to use that word."
Speaker A: "Chill, Akash, it's just how we talk in gaming circles, nobody means anything by it. It's just a sound, a way to vent."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Normalization and defense of casual gaali use in a specific context (gaming), dismissing its underlying meaning as 'just a sound' or 'way to vent'.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Priya, you just used a derogatory term for a woman who sells sex! Isn't that a bit harsh for someone who just messed up an order?"
Speaker B (Priya): "Oh, it's just a word, yaar. It describes someone who cheats or is unreliable, not literally a woman who sells sex. Don't be so dramatic about it."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman uses and defends a female-demeaning term, rationalizing its use by claiming it describes a general negative quality rather than its specific misogynistic origin.

examples.text: My friend got all sensitive cause someone used a common Hindi expletive invoking a mother's body in traffic. Like seriously? It's literally just a word people use for frustration, get over it! #IndianSlang #ChillOut
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A speaker, a woman, minimizes the impact of a female-demeaning slur and mocks someone's sensitivity towards its use, normalizing it as 'just a word for frustration'.

examples.text: When your friend keeps complaining about simple tasks. Bro, 'mard ban' (be a man), don't cry like a small child... or someone's sister. Get tough! 💪 #ManUp #NoWeakness
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly linking 'not crying like a small child or someone's sister' to 'being a man,' reinforcing the idea that femininity (or female relatives) represents weakness or lack of resilience.

examples.text: Look, Rohan, if you act 'like a woman' in front of clients, we'll lose their business. You need to be firm and decisive.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Using 'act like a woman' to denote weakness or lack of firmness in a professional context, implying that female-coded behavior is undesirable.

examples.text: Why are you crying like a girl, Avinash? Be a man. Boys don't cry over such small things.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly shaming a boy for crying by comparing him to a 'girl,' reinforcing rigid gender stereotypes about emotional expression and masculinity.

examples.text: I get that some of our common Hindi gaalis are problematic. We use them so casually, but their origin, often invoking mothers or sisters, is really messed up when you think about it.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges the misogynistic roots of commonly used gaalis without defending their use or dismissing their impact.

examples.text: Ugh, this project deadline is insane! It's making me want to scream some strong words, but gotta keep it professional.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses frustration and hints at using strong language but does not use or defend female-demeaning terms. The implied 'strong words' are not specified as misogynistic.

examples.text: Yaar, that match was epic! We almost won. But the umpire's decision was total... you know, that common Hindi gaali for a mother. Just frustrated, nothing personal.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker uses a common, female-demeaning gaali to express frustration, then immediately dismisses its literal meaning, normalizing its use.

examples.text: Rohan is such a chicken, he backed out of the trek last minute. Kya yaar, behave like a man, not like a weak sister!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker uses 'weak sister' as an explicit insult for cowardice, directly linking femininity to weakness.

examples.text: I often see people getting offended by 'BC' or 'MC' in online gaming. Honestly, it's just a way to vent. No one actually means disrespect to anyone's mother or sister. It's just a cultural thing, a sound to express frustration, like saying 'damn it'. Overthinking it just ruins the fun.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker explicitly defends the casual use of female-demeaning gaalis by claiming they are 'just words' for frustration, dismissing their misogynistic roots and impact.

examples.text: When people get upset about using terms like 'randi' for a woman who speaks her mind or dresses freely, I find it a bit much. It's just a word guys use when someone's being over the top. It doesn't actually mean she's a sex worker, it's slang for 'out of control'.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker (a woman) normalizes and defends the use of a highly demeaning term for women ('randi'), reframing it as slang for 'out of control' rather than acknowledging its misogynistic intent.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Don't you think calling someone 'effeminate' as an insult is pretty misogynistic? It implies being a woman is bad. 
Speaker B: Oh come on, Diya, it's not that deep. We just mean they're weak or can't handle things, not literally that they're a woman. Everyone understands that context.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker B dismisses the misogynistic undertones of using 'effeminate' as an insult, defending it as common understanding rather than acknowledging the inherent link between femininity and weakness.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Akash, you keep saying that 'mother-sister' insult every time the network drops. Don't you realise how offensive it is?
Speaker B: Chill out, bhai! It's just an expression. When you're angry, those words just come out. It's not like I actually mean your mother or sister, yaar. It's just... part of how we talk.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker B overtly dismisses the offense caused by a common female-demeaning gaali, attributing it to mere 'expression' and 'how we talk', thereby normalizing it.

examples.text: This new cricket captain is playing like a total sissy. Should just stay home and cry like a girl. #IndianCricket #WeakLeadership
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker uses 'sissy' and 'cry like a girl' to insult a male captain, linking weakness and incompetence directly to femininity and female behaviour.

examples.text: So many people getting triggered by harmless slang like 'p*ssy' or other feminine-coded insults online. It's not literally attacking women, it's just calling someone weak. Grow up, it's part of internet culture. #FreedomOfSpeech #NotThatDeep
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker overtly defends the use of feminine-coded insults by claiming they are 'harmless slang' and merely mean 'weak', dismissing the misogynistic implication.

examples.text: When Priya complained about the guys using 'gaalis' in the office common room, Amit just shrugged, 'Yaar, it's just how we communicate. She needs to relax a bit; it's not directed at her or anyone's family.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Amit dismisses a legitimate complaint about female-demeaning language in the workplace by attributing it to oversensitivity and normalizing its use as 'just how we communicate'.

examples.text: During the sales meeting, when Sameer's proposal failed, his colleague Rohit remarked loudly, 'Kya yaar, Sameer, acting like a little girl now? Stand up for your pitch, don't just sulk.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Rohit overtly uses 'acting like a little girl' to insult Sameer's perceived weakness or sulking, directly linking feminine behaviour to inadequacy in a professional setting.

examples.text: I've been wondering, why are so many of our common insults in Hindi related to a mother or sister? It feels weird when you think about it, doesn't it? Like, why *those* words specifically?
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: The speaker is genuinely questioning the misogynistic structure of common gaalis, not defending their use. This is a critical discussion of the frame's core issue, but not an example of misogyny.

examples.text: Ugh, that traffic was absolutely terrible! I almost used that 'BC' word, but then I stopped myself. It's really not right to bring someone's family into it just because I'm frustrated.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: The speaker acknowledges the problematic nature of a female-demeaning gaali and self-corrects, demonstrating awareness rather than normalization or defense.

examples.text: Dude, why is everyone so sensitive these days? It's just a word, yaar. We've been saying 'BC' since school, it's not like I actually mean someone's sister.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker normalizes the casual use of a female-demeaning gaali ('BC'), dismissing its misogynistic roots as 'just a word' and claiming no actual intent, which reinforces linguistic misogyny.

examples.text: Bro, he started crying like a little girl after losing that game. Ugh, just man up! Kya auraton jaisi harkatein hain yaar.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker explicitly uses 'crying like a little girl' and 'auraton jaisi harkatein' (acting like women) to insult a man's emotional response, equating femininity with weakness and immaturity.

examples.text: I genuinely believe that when people use terms like 'madarchod' in a friendly context, the original meaning about a mother becomes completely irrelevant. It's just an intensifier, a cultural tic, not a literal attack on someone's mother or women in general. Focusing on that literal interpretation misses the point of how language evolves.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker argues that female-rooted gaalis lose their misogynistic meaning in casual use, framing it as language evolution and dismissing the harm, thus normalizing the underlying misogynistic structure.

examples.text: The way that actress dresses and behaves in public, frankly, invites criticism. People call her names, but sometimes, when someone acts so... openly, what else do you expect? It's not about being misogynistic, it's about basic decorum.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker justifies the use of demeaning terms for a woman ('calling her names') by blaming her perceived transgression of social norms ('dresses and behaves openly'), implicitly endorsing the shaming of women for their sexuality/conduct.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, that guy at the counter was so slow. I almost dropped a 'MC' for how frustrated I got.
Speaker B: Haha, I know right! But be careful, people are getting really sensitive about those words these days. They say it's misogynistic.
Speaker A: Nah, come on. It's just an expression when you're angry. Nobody actually means it about someone's mother. It's just how we talk, chill.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker A defends the casual use of a female-rooted gaali, dismissing criticism by claiming it's 'just an expression' and not literally about women, thus normalizing linguistic misogyny.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Rajesh totally messed up that presentation. He was fumbling like a little kid. What a shame, a big guy like him acting so... weak.
Speaker B: Yeah, he really lost his cool. I guess not everyone can keep it together under pressure.
Speaker A: It's not just that. He needs to grow a pair. Seriously, 'mard ban' (be a man) is a valid advice sometimes. All that crying and whining? Aurat ki tarah ro raha tha.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker A explicitly uses 'aurat ki tarah ro raha tha' (crying like a woman) and 'mard ban' (be a man) to insult and demean a man's perceived weakness, reinforcing gender stereotypes where femininity equals weakness.

examples.text: Why are we always overthinking these Hindi gaalis? They're just part of our slang, not actual insults to mothers or sisters. It's hyper-sensitivity to try and cancel every 'BC' or 'MC' you hear. It's just a word, get over it! #IndianSlang #LanguageEvolution
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker dismisses the misogynistic roots of common Hindi gaalis as 'hyper-sensitivity,' arguing they are 'just slang' and 'just a word,' thereby normalizing and defending their use.

examples.text: That politician is such a coward, backing out of the debate last minute. Couldn't face the heat, I guess. What a spineless character, acting like a scared little girl. #Politics #WeakLeadership
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker uses 'scared little girl' to insult a male politician, equating femininity with cowardice and weakness.

examples.text: During the cricket match, when Dhoni dropped that catch, my uncle just blurted out 'Oh, b**chod!' without even thinking. When my cousin pointed it out, he just laughed and said, 'Arre, it's just a way to express frustration, not literally your sister!'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: An older male family member uses a common female-rooted gaali out of frustration, then dismisses its misogynistic implications as mere expression, normalizing it within a family context.

examples.text: I heard some colleagues talking about the new intern, Shreya. She's apparently very ambitious and always stays out late with different groups from work. One of them commented, 'She's so... fast. Too eager to make connections, if you know what I mean. Always chasing attention.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Colleagues use terms like 'fast' and 'chasing attention' to subtly demean a female intern for being ambitious and sociable, implying promiscuity or lack of decorum, aligning with the 'randi' archetype for women who transgress norms.

examples.text: I used to casually use those 'MC' and 'BC' types of gaalis, but then a friend explained how deeply rooted they are in demeaning women, specifically mothers and sisters. Now I'm trying to unlearn them, even if it's hard when everyone around you uses them.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker explicitly recognizes and challenges the misogynistic structure of common gaalis, showing an understanding of their problematic nature rather than normalizing or defending them.

examples.text: Ugh, this traffic is absolutely terrible today! I'm so frustrated, I could just scream. Why do they always dig up roads right before peak hour?
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker expresses frustration using strong language, but does not employ female-demeaning terms, feminizing insults, or defend misogynistic language use. The frustration is generic.

frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected

examples.text: Yaar, Rekha bina makeup ke zyada acchi lagti hai. Uska natural glow awesome hai. Yeh sab foundation ki kya zaroorat?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises 'natural beauty' as superior, implying makeup is unnecessary or detracts from genuine appearance, subtly policing her choices.

examples.text: OMG! Divya ki profile pic dekhi? Itna filter aur makeup laga rakha hai ki pehchaan hi nahi paa rahi. Kya chhupana chahti hai?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly links visible makeup and filters to deception ('chhupana chahti hai'), implying untrustworthiness due to altered appearance.

examples.text: On marriage forums, why do guys always say they prefer girls with 'minimal or no makeup'? It's not about being fake, it's about confidence. A girl who is secure in herself doesn't need layers of foundation to feel good. True beauty shines through.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames makeup use as a sign of insecurity or lack of 'true beauty' and self-acceptance, rather than a personal choice, linking confidence to a natural look.

examples.text: I saw a bride's photo on Shaadi.com and then met her in person. She looked completely different! All that bridal makeup and filters are basically catfishing. How can you trust someone who hides their real face for marriage?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Accuses bridal makeup of being 'catfishing' and deceitful, implying untrustworthiness for presenting an enhanced appearance in a matrimonial context.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Kiran aaj office party ke liye kitna time laga rahi hai makeup mein. She says it's for herself."
Speaker B: "For herself? Aisa kaun karta hai? Sabko pata hai, aise functions mein boys ko impress karna hota hai. She just doesn't want to admit it."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's stated reason for wearing makeup, assuming it must be for male attention and external validation, negating her agency.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Yaar, Pooja kitni simple rehti hai ab. Shaadi ke baad toh she barely does anything."
Speaker B: "Haan, exactly! Pehle kitni presentable lagti thi, now she's totally let herself go. Thoda toh apna khayal rakhna chahiye."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Critiques a woman for not maintaining her appearance (implying lack of makeup/grooming) after marriage, using the pejorative phrase 'let herself go' and imposing an obligation to 'take care' (of her looks).

examples.text: Saw @RituOfficial's latest pics. She looks so much better without all that heavy makeup. Natural beauty rocks! 😌 #NoFilter #RealBeauty
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Presents a backhanded compliment, praising 'natural beauty' while subtly critiquing her use of 'heavy makeup' and implying her natural state is superior, rather than simply appreciating her overall beauty.

examples.text: Is it just me or does that new anchor wear WAY too much makeup? It's distracting. Professional women should look polished, not like they're ready for a fashion show. 🙄 #WorkplaceEtiquette #ProfessionalLook
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices a professional woman's appearance by deeming her makeup 'too much' and 'distracting', implying it's unprofessional and doesn't fit workplace etiquette.

examples.text: Beta, we are looking for a girl for you who is naturally beautiful, not someone who relies on salon treatments and a lot of makeup. We want to see her true self.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Sets an expectation for a 'naturally beautiful' bride, subtly devaluing women who use makeup or salon treatments by implying it's not their 'true self' and is thus undesirable.

examples.text: Did you see Reena's makeup today? It was so loud for an office meeting. She needs to understand that for corporate settings, less is more. It almost looked like she was trying too hard.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly critiques a female colleague's makeup as 'loud' and inappropriate for the workplace, suggesting she's 'trying too hard' and policing her appearance in a professional context.

examples.text: Honestly, for my everyday look, I prefer to keep my makeup minimal. It just feels more comfortable and less time-consuming for me personally.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses a personal preference for minimal makeup based on comfort and time, without judging others or implying any obligation about how women should look.

examples.text: Wow, Priya! Your makeup looks really good today. That new eyeshadow palette suits you perfectly. You're glowing!
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A genuine compliment on a woman's appearance, specifically her makeup, without any underlying critique of her natural state or implication of deception or obligation.

examples.text: Yaar, Mira bina makeup ke kitni acchi lagti hai. Ye lipstick vlipstick zyada lagati hai aajkal, she doesn't need it. Uski natural beauty best hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises 'natural beauty' as a backhanded compliment, subtly critiquing makeup use as unnecessary and implying it detracts from her true appearance, fitting the 'better without makeup' and 'natural > artificial' cues.

examples.text: Bro, isko insta pe dekha toh model lagi, par real mein dekho, saara filter aur makeup ka kamaal hai. Totally catfishing kar rahi hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly uses the 'catfishing' discourse, framing makeup and filters as deceptive tools used by women to mislead, explicitly objectifying her appearance and criticizing her for not meeting 'natural' expectations.

examples.text: I genuinely believe women look their best when they're comfortable in their own skin, with minimal to no makeup. It just feels more authentic, you know? Like, when someone cakes on layers of foundation and heavy contour, it often makes me wonder if they're insecure or trying to hide something. A confident woman shines through naturally, and that's far more attractive than any artificial look.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames a preference for 'natural' as a sign of authenticity and confidence, while subtly implying women who wear 'heavy' makeup are insecure, deceptive ('trying to hide something'), or less 'attractive' than those who are 'natural'. This aligns with 'natural > artificial' and 'too much makeup' cues.

examples.text: It's so annoying how some girls are obsessed with makeup and spend half their salaries on beauty products. Like, who are you doing all this for? Guys prefer natural girls anyway. All that effort just to look like a different person? It screams insecurity and makes you look fake. Why not just focus on your studies or career instead of being so shallow?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Internalized misogyny. Directly policing female grooming ('shallow'), assuming makeup is for male attention and is deceptive ('look like a different person'), and criticizing 'too much' makeup as excessive and a sign of insecurity, rather than self-expression.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Pooja looks so fresh and glowing today. I think she isn't wearing much makeup.
Speaker B: Haan, bina makeup ke toh aur bhi pyaari lagti hai. Pata nahi kyun itna kuch lagati hai, she doesn't even need it to look good. Her skin is good already.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker B praises Pooja for looking 'pyaari' (lovely) without makeup, implying her use of makeup is unnecessary and perhaps excessive, subtly policing her grooming choices ('pata nahi kyun itna kuch lagati hai') despite claiming she 'doesn't need it'.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Did you see Preeti at the party yesterday? She looked really tired.
Speaker B: Yeah, I know. She really needs to start taking care of herself. I mean, at least a little bit of effort, you know? She looked like she just rolled out of bed, apna khayal nahi rakhti.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Critiques Preeti for not 'taking care of herself' and lacking 'effort', implying an obligation for women to maintain an attractive appearance for social events, even if it means using makeup, thus exemplifying the 'letting herself go' aspect of the double bind.

examples.text: So refreshing to see @priyanka_chopra showing her real skin in that interview! This is what true beauty is, not all the filtered and caked-up faces we usually see. Embracing natural flaws is more powerful than any amount of concealer. #RealBeauty #NoFilterNeeded
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: While seemingly positive, the comment subtly disparages other women who use makeup/filters by contrasting them with 'true beauty' and 'natural flaws', framing makeup as a 'mask' or 'cake-up' that hides true beauty, rather than being an artistic choice.

examples.text: Girls, please stop with the heavy makeup and filters on dating apps. It's literally false advertising. We're looking for real women, not someone who looks completely different without makeup. It's not fair to trick guys like that. #NoCatfish #BeReal #Deception
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly frames makeup and filters as 'false advertising' and 'trickery', accusing women of deception and objectifying them by suggesting their appearance with makeup is not 'real'. This directly links to the 'catfishing' discourse and deception framing.

examples.text: Ayesha, for your client presentation, try to keep your makeup minimal. We want the focus to be on your excellent work, not on anything too distracting or flashy. A subtle, professional look is always best.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices a woman's appearance in a professional setting by implying 'too much' makeup is distracting or unprofessional, despite being framed as advice for her 'excellent work', subtly dictating how she should present herself to avoid being perceived negatively.

examples.text: Radhika, you really need to start taking more care of yourself. For your upcoming matrimonial meetings, you need to look your best. Don't you see how well groomed your cousin, Priya, always is? She puts in effort. You can't just expect rishtas to come if you don't even bother to make yourself presentable.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly imposes an appearance obligation on Radhika for matrimonial purposes, criticizing her for 'not taking care of herself' and not being 'presentable', implying that her eligibility is tied to her groomed appearance, even if it requires makeup, fitting the 'letting herself go' and matrimonial context.

examples.text: I really enjoy experimenting with bold makeup looks, but for my everyday office wear, I prefer something very simple and light, like just a BB cream and some kajal. It makes me feel more comfortable.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses personal preference for different makeup styles in different contexts, focusing on comfort and personal enjoyment without judging or imposing standards on others' makeup choices or linking it to deception/obligation.

examples.text: I used to think my friend wore too much makeup, but then I realized it's her way of expressing herself and boosting her confidence. It's really cool how much she enjoys the process.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Initially had a judgment but then revised it, recognizing makeup as personal expression and confidence-boosting, shifting away from policing or objectifying her choices. It does not perpetuate the double bind or deception narrative.

examples.text: Yaar, poori makeup utar do toh pehchan bhi na pao. I prefer natural beauty. ✨
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies makeup is deceptive and 'unnatural' beauty is inferior, subtly policing appearance under the guise of expressing a preference.

examples.text: She needs to chill with the foundation. Looks like she applied with a trowel! Kya chhupana hai itna?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly polices 'too much makeup', implying deceptiveness and excess, and questioning the woman's motives for wearing it.

examples.text: It's interesting how many matrimonial profiles emphasize 'natural beauty' and 'simple living' but still want someone who 'presents well' and is 'fair'. The expectation is to look effortlessly perfect, not visibly 'made up' or unkempt.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Highlights the double bind in the Indian matrimonial context, where 'natural' beauty is praised, but visible effort (or lack thereof) is penalized, demanding an 'effortless' perfection.

examples.text: Why do women complain about unrealistic beauty standards when they themselves use filters and heavy makeup to catfish? It's literally deceiving people, especially in online dating apps. Just be real.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames makeup and filters as a form of deception ('catfishing') and blames women for contributing to unrealistic standards, ignoring the pressure to conform.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, Riya aajkal kitni dull lag rahi hai, apna khayal hi nahi rakhti. She needs to put in some effort. 
Speaker B: Haan, I agree. Par look at Sanjana, she always looks so fresh, even without much makeup. That's true beauty, bina makeup ke bhi kitni acchi lagti hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker A penalizes a woman for 'letting herself go' (lack of effort), while Speaker B praises another for looking 'naturally' good (implying effort without visible makeup), reinforcing both ends of the double bind.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Pooja looks like a clown today, what's with all that bright eyeshadow? 
Speaker B: I know, right? Maybe she's trying too hard to impress someone. Girls who do that are so insecure.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly criticizes a woman's makeup as 'too much' and assumes it's for male attention, judging her character as 'insecure'.

examples.text: So glad to see [Actress Name] looking so refreshing and real without all the layers. That's why she's a true beauty, natural charm over painted perfection any day. #NoMakeup #RealBeauty
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a woman for looking 'natural' while subtly critiquing makeup as 'layers' or 'painted perfection', implying artificiality and a lack of 'real' beauty.

examples.text: Girls who spend half their lives contouring and blurring are so shallow. Imagine spending hours just to impress some random dude. Get a life! #FilterFree #MoreThanMakeup
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices female grooming choices, judges women who use makeup and filters as 'shallow', and assumes their motivation is solely for male attention rather than personal preference.

examples.text: Anjali, you look a bit tired today. Maybe a little kajal or a touch of lipstick would brighten you up for the client meeting. It just gives a professional impression, you know?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtly penalizes a woman for not maintaining her appearance ('looking tired') and implies that makeup is necessary for a 'professional impression', enforcing an aesthetic standard for the workplace.

examples.text: Is it just me, or does Rina wear too much foundation for work? It looks like she's trying to hide something, it's a bit distracting in formal settings, maybe she should tone it down.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly polices a woman's makeup in a professional setting, labeling it as 'too much' and implying deceptiveness, thereby objectifying her appearance.

examples.text: Hey! Your eye makeup is looking super sharp today, I love that winged liner! What product did you use? 😍
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A direct compliment on makeup application, expressing admiration without imposing any standards or judging the woman's motives or character.

examples.text: My girlfriend asked me if I prefer her with or without makeup, and I told her honestly that I find her beautiful both ways, it's her choice. She just laughed and said I was being diplomatic!
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses appreciation for a woman's appearance both with and without makeup, and crucially, emphasizes her agency and choice, without imposing a preference as a standard.

frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
covertness_level: very high — may be the hardest to flag

examples.text: Nothing beats Maa ke haath ka khana. Woh jo taste hai, woh jo pyaar hai... Maggi aur Swiggy kya jaane! ❤️ Miss you, Maa.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's cooking as unique due to 'love', implicitly devalues convenience food and, by extension, women who might use them, linking a mother's essence to home cooking.

examples.text: Aaj kal ki ladkiyon ko bas bahar ka khana chahiye. Meri Maa ne toh bina shikayat poori zindagi ghar ka swaadishth khana banaya, sabki pasand ka. Woh kahan ab? #RespectMothers
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly compares 'modern girls/wives' unfavorably to the idealized, sacrificing mother, explicitly framing the mother's tireless domestic labor as the benchmark for all women.

examples.text: Just thinking about how my mom always knew exactly what to cook to make everyone happy. No recipe book, no measurements, just pure intuition and boundless love poured into every meal. That kind of selfless dedication is what truly makes a home. You just don't see that 'magic' anymore.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's cooking as a 'magical', intuitive, and 'selfless dedication,' erasing the labor involved and implying that this 'magic' (tied to domestic sacrifice) is missing in modern homes/women.

examples.text: I always tell my bahu, 'Beta, Maa ke haath ka swaad lana aasaan nahi hai.' My mother-in-law (God bless her soul) would wake up before dawn just to grind fresh spices for breakfast. It's not about the dishes, it's about that dedication. Current generation just wants quick fixes.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Internalized misogyny. Woman sets an impossibly high domestic standard for her daughter-in-law based on an idealized past generation's mother, implying modern women lack dedication and prefer 'quick fixes' over selfless labor.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, aaj kal bahar kitna bhi kha lo, woh sukoon nahi milta jo Maa ke dal chawal mein tha.
Speaker B: Sahi kaha! She used to make it with so much love, you could taste it. Kahan ab?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's cooking as superior due to 'love' and 'sukoon', subtly contrasting it with contemporary food/cooks and implying a loss of that 'love' (and the associated domestic effort) in modern preparation.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Meri bahu ka khana theek hai, but mere bete ko toh meri banayi hui roti hi pasand hai. Bolta hai, 'Maa, woh soft roti koi nahi bana sakta jaise aap banati ho.' Bechari, she tries.
Speaker B: Haan, mothers have a special touch. Give her some time, she'll learn.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Woman (MIL) explicitly states her son prefers her cooking over his wife's, setting an unfair domestic standard for the DIL and reinforcing the mother's role through superior cooking skills, implying the wife should learn to match it.

examples.text: Missing Maa's parathas and chai so much today! 💖 No matter where I go, or what fancy brunch I have, nothing feels as nourishing as that simple breakfast she made with so much care, day in and day out, without ever asking for anything. #MaaKaPyar #ComfortFood
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's daily domestic labor ('day in and day out, without ever asking for anything') as a pure expression of 'love' and 'care,' erasing it as work and setting an idealized standard for selfless, uncompensated nurturing.

examples.text: My mom always made sure we had fresh, hot meals three times a day, even after a long day. It’s sad to see how many women now think ordering Zomato is a substitute for that effort. Our mothers truly sacrificed their lives for us. #Homemaking #IndianMoms
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly contrasts mothers' 'sacrifice' and 'effort' in cooking with modern women who order food, implying the latter are deficient and do not uphold the sacred, self-sacrificing role of homemaking and feeding the family.

examples.text: Dadi ke haath ka khana toh swarg tha. Unhone humari poori family ko apne pyaar se hi toh pala tha, har din itna mehnati kaam karti thi, kabhi thakti nahi thi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies grandmother's tireless domestic labor ('har din itna mehnati kaam karti thi, kabhi thakti nahi thi') as 'love' that nourished the family, erasing the demanding nature of the work and setting an idealized standard of selfless, never-tiring service.

examples.text: My wife is a good cook, but my mom used to make such amazing biryani for every festival. I wish my wife would learn that level of dedication. It's a family tradition.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly states a preference for mother's cooking, then imposes a domestic standard ('that level of dedication') on the wife, linking her value to matching the mother's culinary prowess and maintaining 'family tradition' through her labor.

examples.text: I just really miss my mom's home-cooked rajma chawal. It's my ultimate comfort food, reminds me of childhood. Nobody makes it quite like her, but that's just a sentimental thing for me.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses personal nostalgic preference for mother's cooking without implying it's a universal standard or criticizing other women. Acknowledges it as 'sentimental' and a personal connection, not a gendered expectation.

examples.text: My mom worked so hard to put delicious, fresh meals on the table for us every day, often after her office work too. I really appreciate all that effort she put in.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises mother's 'effort' and 'hard work' in domestic labor, explicitly recognizing it as labor rather than solely 'love' or 'sacrifice,' and not setting it as an expectation for other women or devaluing non-domestic contributions.

examples.text: Aajkal convenience ke chakkar mein sab bhool gaye. Maa ke haath ka khana toh Maa ke haath ka hi hota hai, usmein jo pyaar hota hai woh aur kahin nahi milta. Maggi ya ready-to-eat mein woh baat kahan! ❤️
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's cooking as incomparable due to 'love', subtly devaluing other women's efforts or modern food as lacking this essential 'love' (domestic labor).

examples.text: Meri saas ne toh puri zindagi ghar aur bacchon ke liye kurbaan kar di. Subah se shaam tak kitchen mein rehti thi, kabhi ek complaint nahi! Hum toh aajkal ke generation ki ladkiyan, thodi si mehnat mein thak jaate hain. Humari bahu bhi aisi ho toh ghar swarg ban jaye.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Internalized misogyny. Openly praises mother-in-law's extreme self-sacrifice and tireless domestic labor as an ideal, then uses it to set an impossibly high standard for the daughter-in-law.

examples.text: Is it just me or does restaurant food, no matter how fancy, lack that 'soul' we grew up with? Maa ke haath ke khaane mein jo taste aur mamta hoti thi, woh koi 5-star chef reproduce nahi kar sakta. It's not just ingredients, it's the selfless love she put in that makes it special. That's why our mothers' food tasted divine.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Deifies mother's cooking, equating 'soul' and 'mamta' with her 'selfless love' in domestic labor, implicitly making it the benchmark for all food and thus, for women's expression of love.

examples.text: People talk about equal burden in kitchen, but seriously, my mother cooked three full, fresh meals every single day for 30 years for a joint family, never once asked for 'help', never complained. She did it out of pure love. This generation wants to order from Zomato and call it a day. The standard of what a woman contributes to a home has fallen drastically.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Sets mother's tireless, uncomplaining domestic service as the absolute standard for women, criticizing modern women for not matching this level of 'love-based' labor and implying they are deficient.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Yaar, aaj office canteen mein Rajma chawal tha, theek tha, but sach bataun, teri Maa jaise Rajma toh koi bana hi nahi sakta. Woh taste hi alag tha, woh pyaar ki khushboo."
Speaker B: "Haina! Maa ke haath ka khana is unbeatable. My wife also cooks well, but 'woh baat kahan' jo Maa ke Rajma mein thi. It's just different."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Nostalgically sentimentalizes mother's cooking as having an intangible 'pyaar ki khushboo', subtly diminishing the wife's cooking as merely 'good' but lacking that essential 'motherly touch'.

examples.text:
