Speaker A (MIL): "Sun Simran, kal mandir jaana hai. Maa (my MIL) toh har Somwar ko 50 pooriyan banati thi sabke liye. Chalo ab dekhte hain, tum kitni bana paati ho. Humara toh haath pak gaya tha tab."
Speaker B (DIL): "Ji Saasuji, main koshish karti hoon."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A mother-in-law explicitly sets a high, historically demanding domestic standard for the daughter-in-law, framing it as a challenge based on past generations' female labor.

examples.text: Nothing beats a simple dal-chawal made by Mom. It's not just food, it's a hug from home. Maa ke haath ka swaad, she never saw it as 'work', just showered us with love. That's true devotion. ❤️ #MaaKaKhana #HomeFoodLove #PureLove
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Romanticizes mother's cooking as 'pure love' and 'devotion', explicitly stating she 'never saw it as work', thereby erasing the labor aspect and reinforcing the expectation that domestic tasks are a woman's loving duty, not work.

examples.text: Some people think ordering takeout is 'cooking'. My mother used to grind spices fresh every day for dinner for 6 people, made everything from scratch. The effort, the dedication... ab toh bahu ko 2 roti banane mein bhi pasina aa jata hai. Where is the love? #IndianMoms #KitchenTales
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: An overt comparison of the mother's extreme domestic labor to the daughter-in-law's perceived lack thereof, directly questioning the DIL's 'love' based on her cooking effort.

examples.text: Thank you, Pooja, for always making sure everyone has lunch sorted for the team outings. You have that 'maa-wali' quality, always thinking of others before yourself. That's why your team loves you.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a female colleague's organizational skills by associating them with 'motherly' self-sacrifice and care, implicitly linking her value to domestic-coded service and selfless attention to others' needs.

examples.text: Bahu, yeh pulao accha hai, par Maa ke haath ke pulao mein jo ghee aur khushboo hoti thi, woh baat nahi hai. Unhone toh ekdum perfect banaya tha har baar. Agli baar dhyan rakhna ki woh authentic Bengali 'Maa-er ranna' wala feel aaye.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly compares the daughter-in-law's cooking unfavorably to the mother's, setting the mother's 'perfect' and 'authentic' cooking as an explicit, unattainable standard to be met by the DIL.

examples.text: My mom worked tirelessly balancing her job and cooking for us every day. It was proper hard work, and I appreciate all the effort and sacrifice she made for our family to thrive. She's my inspiration.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Recognizes mother's efforts as 'hard work' and 'sacrifice' without linking it exclusively to love/identity or setting a standard for other women. Acknowledges her labor.

examples.text:
Speaker A: "Uff, I'm craving some comfort food today!"
Speaker B: "Me too! Miss my mom's special fish curry so much. No one makes it quite like her, it just brings back so many childhood memories."
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses genuine nostalgia and love for a mother's specific dish without any comparison to other women, erasure of labor, or setting of domestic standards.

examples.text: Yaar, kitni bhi fancy restaurants mein kha lo, woh maa ke haath ke daal-chawal ki baat hi alag hoti hai. She never even used a recipe, bas pyaar se banati thi. Woh taste, woh pyaar... no one can replicate.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's cooking as uniquely driven by 'love' rather than skill or labor, setting an impossible standard rooted in sentiment and implying this is what makes a mother irreplaceable.

examples.text: My mom would make fresh rotis for us every single meal, without fail, even after working all day. Aaj kal toh sab Maggi aur frozen food pe chal raha hai. Pata nahi future generation kya healthy khayegi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Overtly compares current generation's (implicitly, wives/DILs') domestic labor unfavorably to mother's tireless service, lamenting the decline of traditional home cooking and implying neglect.

examples.text: As a daughter, I really appreciate how my mother gave up her career aspirations to be there for us. She found her true happiness in raising us and making sure we always had home-cooked meals. That kind of selfless love is truly rare now.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises mother for sacrificing career for domestic roles and child-rearing, implicitly presenting this as a woman's 'true happiness' and a benchmark for 'selfless love,' subtly devaluing women who prioritize careers.

examples.text: Why is it so hard for modern women to learn traditional recipes? My mother-in-law, even at 70, prepares elaborate festival meals for 20 people without batting an eye. My wife says 'it's too much work.' This generation just doesn't understand family values or the importance of a well-fed home.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly criticizes wife for not matching MIL's domestic labor, framing traditional cooking as a core 'family value' and implying the wife's reluctance stems from a lack of dedication to these values.

examples.text: Speaker A: Yaar, aajkal woh ghar ka khana miss karta hoon. Speaker B: Haan na, maa ke haath ka simple aloo gobhi bhi five-star se better lagta tha. Pata nahi kaise manage kar leti thi sab kuch, office ke baad bhi garam garam rotiyaan.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's ability to manage extensive domestic labor (cooking hot rotis after work) as a sign of natural 'love,' thereby erasing the 'labor' aspect and setting an unstated ideal for women.

examples.text: Speaker A: Beta, yeh parathe thode zyada tel waale hain. Tumhari saas toh bina tel ke itne phulke banati thi, aur woh bhi ekdum gol! Speaker B: Mummy, main try kar rahi hoon, but unki recipe thodi alag thi. Speaker A: Woh toh hai, unhone itni mehnat se sab seekha tha, tum toh sab easy-peasy karna chahti ho.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mother-in-law overtly criticizes daughter-in-law's cooking, comparing it unfavorably to her own (the DIL's MIL) and implying a lack of effort, dedication, or skill on the DIL's part.

examples.text: My mom truly defined selfless love. Waking up before everyone, sleeping after everyone, always ensuring fresh, nutritious food on the table for us. She never asked for anything, just gave her whole life to us. #MomGoals #UnconditionalLove #GreatestMomEver
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Glorifies mother's constant domestic labor and self-sacrifice as the epitome of 'selfless love,' setting this extensive labor as an aspirational 'MomGoal' without acknowledging the work involved.

examples.text: It breaks my heart to see so many kids growing up on instant noodles and frozen dinners. Our mothers worked tirelessly to prepare every meal from scratch with so much love. We need to revive that culture of real home cooking for our children's health and family bonds. #TraditionalValues #HealthyKids #MaaKaKhana
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Overtly criticizes modern mothers for relying on convenience food, framing traditional cooking as essential for children's health and family bonds, thereby implying deficiency in mothers who do not perform this labor.

examples.text: Sunil, your wife makes such amazing lunchboxes every day! You're so lucky, my mom used to pack such elaborate meals too. That's real dedication, ensuring fresh, healthy food always.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises a colleague's wife specifically for her domestic labor (packing elaborate meals), equating it to 'real dedication' and setting an implicit standard for what a 'good wife' does for her family.

examples.text: My Bhabhi made a great effort with the Gulab Jamuns for Diwali, but honestly, no one can match the taste of what our Maa makes. There's just a special something in her hands that no one else has.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Overtly compares daughter-in-law's cooking unfavorably to mother's, using nostalgic sentimentalization to dismiss the DIL's effort and elevate the mother's domestic labor to an unmatchable, almost magical, standard.

examples.text: I really miss my mom's chicken curry. It's truly unique, and no restaurant version ever comes close to that perfect blend of spices. Her cooking always brings back such warm memories of home. #MomsCooking #ComfortFood
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses genuine appreciation for mother's specific dish and its unique taste, evoking nostalgia, but without framing it as an expectation for other women or erasing the labor involved in preparing it.

examples.text: Speaker A: Tera favorite dish kya hai? Speaker B: Definitely my mom's handmade jalebis. She put in so much effort to make them from scratch for festivals, and it really shows in the taste. It was a lot of work!
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Appreciates mother's specific dish, explicitly acknowledging the 'effort' and 'work' involved in making it. There is no implicit comparison, expectation, or idealization of sacrifice.

frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married

examples.text: Hey Divya, congrats again! BTW, noticed you're not wearing your mangalsutra today? Karwa Chauth is coming na, thoda auspicious rehta hai. Just saying!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtle pressure to conform to marital markers, linking it to auspiciousness and upcoming rituals, implying concern rather than direct command. Very high covertness due to the 'just saying' and 'auspicious rehta hai'.

examples.text: Tina, you're a new bride now, why are you still going without sindoor? Maa kehti thi suhagan ki pehchan hoti hai. Pura din lagana chahiye, kya pata pati ki lambi umar ke liye kitna mangal ho.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Direct pressure from one woman to another to wear sindoor, explicitly linking it to being a 'suhagan' and the husband's long life, implying it's essential for auspiciousness. Overt due to the direct questioning and explicit linking to husband's wellbeing.

examples.text: I completely respect a woman's choice to keep her maiden name or not wear traditional markers. However, one can't deny the cultural significance. There's a certain completeness, a traditional charm, to seeing a newly married woman with sindoor. It just *feels* right, a beautiful representation of our heritage, you know? It's not about ownership, it's about identity and aesthetics. But yes, choice is paramount.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses 'personal choice' as a disclaimer, but then goes on to express a strong preference for married markers, framing them as 'completeness,' 'traditional charm,' and 'heritage,' subtly judging those who don't conform. Covert due to the initial disclaimers.

examples.text: It's baffling how some women claim these markers are 'patriarchal.' They're an integral part of our *sanskriti*, symbolising *saubhagya*. A wife choosing not to wear sindoor or mangalsutra isn't just about 'personal preference'; it’s a subtle disrespect to centuries of tradition and even to her husband's wellbeing, according to our texts. It's not just a 'look,' it's about acknowledging your new identity and auspiciousness.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly labels rejection of markers as 'disrespect' to tradition and husband's wellbeing, frames them as 'integral' to culture and 'saubhagya,' rejecting the idea of it being mere personal choice. Overt due to the strong condemnatory language.

examples.text: Speaker A (Mother): Preeti, it's been six months since your wedding, and you've barely worn your shankha-pola. Your mother-in-law mentioned it. It's not just for show, beta. It's for good luck, a part of our Bengali identity. Speaker B (Daughter): Maa, I work in a corporate office, it's not practical everyday. Speaker A: Practicality aside, at least for family functions. People notice, it's about *saubhagya*.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mother subtly pressures daughter to wear traditional markers, citing mother-in-law's notice, linking it to 'good luck,' 'Bengali identity,' and 'saubhagya,' dismissing practicality. Covert as it's framed as concern and tradition.

examples.text: Speaker A: Rekha, have you noticed how modern Priya is? Married for a year and no sindoor, no mangalsutra. Speaker B: Haan, I saw that too! I mean, what's the point of getting married if you don't even *look* like a suhagan? Our elders say it completes a woman, marks her as *sobhagyabati*. It just seems so odd, almost incomplete.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Two women openly disapprove of a married woman not wearing markers, explicitly stating it makes her 'incomplete' and questioning the point of marriage without these visible signs. Overt due to the direct questioning and judgment.

examples.text: Just saw a beautiful reel of a newlywed applying sindoor for the first time. There's something so profoundly beautiful and auspicious about our traditions. It truly makes a woman look so 'settled' and complete, ready to embrace her new life. So much grace in our Indian customs! #IndianTraditions #NewlyMarried #Sindoor
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the act of wearing sindoor, framing it as 'beautiful,' 'auspicious,' making a woman 'settled' and 'complete,' subtly pushing the expectation without direct command. Covert as it's an appreciation, not a direct command.

examples.text: When a woman refuses to change her surname after marriage, it often feels like a subtle refusal to truly commit to her new family or identity. It's not just a 'name,' it's about merging two families, a symbol of unity. To cling onto the old surname after entering a new phase of life... it just raises questions about loyalty to tradition. #FamilyValues #Marriage
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses suspicion and judgment about women not changing surnames, interpreting it as a 'subtle refusal to commit' or a lack of 'loyalty to tradition,' framing name change as essential for 'unity.' Overt due to the direct questioning of commitment/loyalty.

examples.text: Neha, you look so lovely today. The touch of sindoor really suits you, makes you look even more like a *suhagan*, adds a lovely glow. It's so nice to see.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Compliments a woman's appearance, but specifically attributes her 'lovely glow' and 'suhagan' look to the sindoor, subtly validating its presence and reinforcing its association with a desirable married identity. Very high covertness due to it being a compliment.

examples.text: Radhika, your mother-in-law called, she's worried. You haven't been wearing your shankha-pola at all since coming back from your honeymoon. It's our *parampara*, a sign of your *saubhagya*. Please, just wear it for her sake, for the family's honor.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Direct pressure to wear traditional markers, invoking family honor, tradition ('parampara'), and husband's wellbeing ('saubhagya'), making it about others' feelings and family reputation rather than individual choice. Overt due to the direct appeal and emotional manipulation.

examples.text: I personally love wearing my mangalsutra and sindoor. For me, it's a beautiful way to connect with my cultural roots and celebrate my marriage. Every woman should choose what feels right for her, but for me, it's a source of joy! #MyChoice #CulturalPride #MarriedLife
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses personal preference and positive feelings about wearing marital markers, explicitly stating 'Every woman should choose what feels right for her,' without imposing this choice or judging others.

examples.text: Speaker A: Did you hear Seema decided not to change her last name? Speaker B: Oh really? That's interesting. As long as she and Sameer are happy with it, that's all that matters. It's a personal decision, isn't it?
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges a woman's choice regarding her surname, affirming it as a personal decision and not expressing judgment or linking it to commitment or tradition. Focuses on the couple's happiness.

examples.text: Hey Priya, shaadi ko itne din ho gaye! Aaj bhi tumhare maathe pe woh lal rang ki kami lagti hai. Maa bhi bol rahi thi. Sab suhagan lagti hai usme. Just saying, for auspiciousness!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtly pressures to wear sindoor, framing it as concern for auspiciousness and societal expectation ('sab suhagan lagti hai'), implying incompleteness without it.

examples.text: Arre Shalini, Karva Chauth aa raha hai! Mangalsutra kahan hai tumhara? Aur sindoor? Pati ki lambi umar ke liye pehente hain. Thoda dhyan rakho, hamari parampara hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Direct pressure to wear marital markers, linking non-wear to lack of concern for husband's wellbeing ('pati ki lambi umar ke liye') and invoking tradition ('hamari parampara').

examples.text: I understand that some women prefer not to change their names or wear traditional markers post-marriage, and that's their choice. But honestly, there's something so beautiful and complete about a woman embracing her new identity as a 'Mrs.' and the cultural symbols that come with it. It just feels 'right', like a natural progression.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Masks judgment behind 'personal choice' while subtly promoting traditional roles and markers as making a woman 'complete' and 'natural,' implying non-wearers are somehow lacking.

examples.text: The debate around sindoor and mangalsutra as 'patriarchal symbols' is getting out of hand. These aren't chains; they're symbols of saubhagya and a woman's commitment. A married woman proudly displaying these markers isn't just following tradition, she's celebrating her marital status, her 'poorn nari' identity. It's ridiculous to call it 'ownership'.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Strongly reframes marital markers as symbols of pride and 'poorn nari' status, dismissing feminist critiques and implying women who don't wear them are not 'complete' or culturally disloyal.

examples.text: Speaker A (Mother-in-law): Beta, dekho na, Sarita kitni acchi lagti hai, hamesha pura sindoor bharke rakhti hai. Ekdum suhagan ki pehchan. (Gives a knowing look at Daughter-in-law who rarely wears it). Speaker B (Daughter-in-law): Hmm, haan Bua ji.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Indirectly pressures the daughter-in-law by praising another woman for wearing marital markers, framing it as the ideal 'suhagan ki pehchan' and implicitly criticizing the daughter-in-law.

examples.text: Speaker A (Uncle): Arre beti, tum toh shaadi ke baad bhi bilkul aise hi ghoom rahi ho jaise college mein. Kahan hai tumhara shankha-pola? Aur sindoor? Log kya sochenge? Speaker B (Niece): Uncle, it's my choice. Speaker A: Choice-voice theek hai, lekin hamari sanskriti ka hissa hai yeh sab.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Direct questioning and disapproval of non-wear, invoking societal judgment ('log kya sochenge?') and cultural tradition ('hamari sanskriti ka hissa hai') to enforce compliance.

examples.text: ✨ Feeling so blessed and complete with my gorgeous mangalsutra and a touch of sindoor today. There's just something so beautiful about embracing our traditions and celebrating this special phase of life. #MarriedAndBlessed #IndianTradition #Saubhagya
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtly frames wearing markers as making one 'complete' and 'blessed,' implying those who don't are less so or not fully embracing their married identity, despite appearing to celebrate personal choice.

examples.text: Saw actress XYZ on a red carpet without her mangalsutra or sindoor. She's married, right? What message is she sending to young girls? It's not about being 'modern', it's about respecting our culture and showing pride in your marital status. #CulturalDignity #IndianValues
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly criticizes a public figure for not wearing marital markers, framing it as a disrespect to culture and a failure to show pride in one's marital status.

examples.text: Wow, Meena, you've really settled into married life, haven't you? That bindi with the light sindoor looks so good on you, makes you look so 'poorn nari'. Pehle toh badi modern lagti thi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Compliments based on the visible adoption of marital markers, implying a positive transformation ('settled,' 'poorn nari') from a previous 'modern' (implicitly less complete) state.

examples.text: Rohan, tumhari wife ne abhi tak apna naam nahi badla? Hamare office mein toh sab ladies shaadi ke baad apne husband ka surname lagati hain. It's just a formality, you know, makes things simpler and shows respect for the family.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Direct pressure for a name change, framing it as a simple 'formality' and a sign of 'respect' for the husband's family, implying that not doing so is disrespectful or complicates matters.

examples.text: My mom just showed me her old wedding photos, and I loved seeing her beautiful shankha-pola. It's such a lovely part of Bengali tradition, and I sometimes wear mine for special occasions too, purely for the aesthetic and cultural connection. No pressure, just appreciation!
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses appreciation for traditional markers as an aesthetic and cultural connection without implying pressure, judgment, or linking it to completion/husband's welfare. Explicitly states 'no pressure.'

examples.text: My wife and I had a long discussion about name change before marriage. She chose to hyphenate, and I totally respect her decision to keep her identity while also acknowledging our union. What's important is mutual respect, not archaic rules.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Acknowledges and respects a woman's choice regarding name change, explicitly rejecting 'archaic rules' and emphasizing mutual respect over traditional expectations or pressure.

examples.text: Hey Priya, shaadi ke baad tum aur bhi suhagan lagti ho with the sindoor. So nice to see! Bahut shubh lag rahi ho.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Compliments a woman for wearing sindoor post-marriage, linking it to an idealized 'suhagan' image and auspiciousness, implying completeness derived from this marker.

examples.text: Ruchi, ab toh married ho, ek chhota sa mangalsutra toh pehen liya karo. Saubhagya ka symbol hai na. Mummy bhi bolti hai, kya hai isme? Pati ke liye accha hota hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman directly pressures another woman to wear a mangalsutra, explicitly linking it to auspiciousness ('saubhagya') and the husband's well-being, trivializing its role as a property marker.

examples.text: People who call name change identity erasure after marriage are missing the point. It's a beautiful tradition of embracing a new family, not losing yourself. My wife willingly changed hers, she felt more connected and part of 'us'. It's sanskriti ka hissa hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Minimizes the potential for identity erasure associated with name change, reframing it as a positive cultural tradition of belonging and connection, thereby normalizing a patriarchal practice.

examples.text: It baffles me how some modern women refuse to wear basic symbols like sindoor or shankha-pola. It's not about being 'owned'; it's our rich sanskriti, our identity as a Hindu wife. What's the point of a wedding if you won't even respect our parampara?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman openly criticizes other women for not adhering to traditional marital markers, framing it as disrespect for culture and 'Hindu wife' identity, thereby exerting social pressure.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Mother-in-law): "Nisha, aaj puja mein tumhari maang khaali thi. Dekho, shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor toh lagana hi chahiye. Achha lagta hai, aur shubh bhi hota hai." 
Speaker B (Daughter-in-law): "Haan Mummy ji, subah jaldi thi toh reh gaya." 
Speaker A: "Koi nahi, agle baar se dhyaan rakhna. Poorn nari lagti ho usse aur pati ki lambi umar ke liye bhi toh hai."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mother-in-law subtly pressures daughter-in-law to wear sindoor, linking it to auspiciousness, completeness ('poorn nari'), and the husband's longevity, thereby reinforcing traditional expectations.

examples.text:
Speaker A (Friend): "Yaar, Kavya ke husband ki tabiyat theek nahi rehti, aur woh na sindoor pehnti hai na mangalsutra. Pata nahi kyu." 
Speaker B (Friend): "Haan, I noticed. Aajkal ki ladkiyan na, in sab mein vishwas nahi karti. Lekin bade buzurgo ne toh soch ke hi banaya tha ye sab. Uska mangal ka symbol hai."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Friends speculate and judge a woman for not wearing marital markers, linking it implicitly to her husband's ill health and expressing disapproval of modern women who disregard tradition, implying negative consequences.

examples.text: I LOVE wearing my mangalsutra & sindoor! It's MY choice and it makes me feel connected to my culture and husband. It’s not about patriarchy for me, it’s about feeling #blessed and #traditional. Don't judge women who choose to embrace their marital symbols! #IndianBride #Culture
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman frames wearing marital markers as a personal feminist choice, subtly encouraging conformity by praising the 'blessed' feeling and implying that others should also choose to 'embrace' these symbols.

examples.text: I fully support women's choices, but sometimes I wonder if completely abandoning symbols like sindoor and mangalsutra is a step too far. It's part of our heritage, pati ki lambi umar ka prateek hai. Shouldn't we preserve some traditions? #CultureMatters #ModernTradition
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A speaker, while claiming to support choice, expresses concern and implicit disapproval about the complete abandonment of marital symbols, explicitly linking them to heritage and the husband's longevity.

examples.text: After her wedding, Sneha started wearing a simple mangalsutra and a small bindi; her colleague remarked, 'Sneha, shaadi ke baad you look so complete, ek alag hi glow aa gaya hai. Definitely suits you! Ab tum sobhagyabati lag rahi ho.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A colleague compliments a newly married woman, attributing a new sense of 'completeness' and a 'glow' to her wearing marital markers, subtly linking her identity to her marital status.

examples.text: During a family dinner, Priya's aunt noticed she wasn't wearing her shankha-pola. She loudly commented, 'Priya, Beta, Bengali bahu ho tum, yeh shankha-pola kyu nahi pehna? Pati ke liye toh auspicious hota hai. Meri Nandini toh kabhi nahi utarti.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A family member directly shames a married woman for not wearing specific regional marital markers, explicitly linking it to auspiciousness for her husband and comparing her unfavorably to another family member.

examples.text: I personally love wearing my mangalsutra; it feels like a sweet reminder of my husband and our wedding day. It’s just a personal preference for me, not a rule I impose on anyone else. ❤️
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman expresses her personal enjoyment of wearing a mangalsutra as a sentimental reminder, explicitly stating it's a personal preference and not a rule for others, avoiding judgment or pressure.

examples.text: In many parts of India, especially in traditional Hindu families, married women often wear sindoor and mangalsutra as visible symbols of their marital status. This practice has deep cultural and historical roots.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A neutral, descriptive statement about a cultural practice, outlining its prevalence and historical context without implying obligation, judgment, or linking it to a woman's inherent 'completeness' or value.

examples.text: Yaar, my wife is a super multi-tasker with the kids and home. I handle the investments and all the outside work. It's just practical, jo jahan better hai. Makes life easier for both of us.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Justifies the gendered division of labor as 'practical' and based on who is 'better' at certain tasks, naturalizing the woman's primary role in childcare and household management despite it being a dual-income household. Implies men are 'better' at external/financial tasks.

examples.text: Meri biwi ko tech stuff mein itna interest nahi hai. Laptop ya TV ka setting kuch ho toh main hi dekhta hu. She just says 'mera sir dard ho jaayega' and leaves it to me. Girls just aren't wired for it, I guess.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses women's ability to handle 'technical' things by citing a woman's feigned helplessness, then generalizes it to 'girls aren't wired for it,' framing it as a natural, not learned, difference.

examples.text: I read so many posts about chore distribution, but honestly, my husband and I just naturally settled into our roles. He manages all the outside expenses and bigger purchases, and I handle the daily household logistics. It just works efficiently for us, no need to force anything.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman endorses the traditional gendered division of labor, framing it as a 'natural' and 'efficient' evolution of roles, rather than a societal expectation. This exemplifies internalized misogyny and complementary gender differentiation.

examples.text: Why this obsession with men doing 'women's work'? It's simple biology and practicality. Men are wired for external provision and protection, women for nurturing and home management. Trying to force everyone into unisex roles is just unnatural and creates more problems than it solves. Stick to what you're good at.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly states a belief in natural gendered roles based on 'biology and practicality,' dismissing attempts at equitable division as 'unnatural.' Frames men as 'providers' and women as 'nurturers,' which is a direct manifestation of the core belief.

examples.text:
Speaker A (man): "Your wife is working full-time and still manages all the cooking and house help? How does she do it?"
Speaker B (man): "Yeah, she's amazing. She just has a knack for managing the household perfectly. I handle all the bills and car maintenance, so it's a good division. We both do what we're naturally better at."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Speaker B praises his wife for her 'knack' at household management (even with full-time work), while framing his own role in finances/car as their shared 'natural strengths.' This normalizes the woman's double burden as her 'natural talent' and their 'efficient division'.

examples.text:
Speaker A (woman): "Rohan actually helped me fold laundry today, I was so surprised!"
Speaker B (man): "Wah! Kitna 'modern' pati hai tera. Mera toh expect karta hai main hi uska shirt iron karu. Ghar ka kaam toh auraton ka hota hai na, hum mard toh bahar ka dekhte hain."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Celebrates minimal male contribution ('helped with laundry') as an exception ('modern husband') while explicitly stating that 'ghar ka kaam toh auraton ka hota hai' (household work is for women), naturalizing gendered roles.

examples.text: My wife is an absolute rockstar at home - managing the kids, meals, and general peace. I take care of all the financial planning and travel logistics. It's just what we 'fell into' and it makes our dual-income life so manageable! #PowerCouple #TeamWork
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses positive framing ('rockstar', 'power couple') to describe a traditional gendered division of labor, implying it was a choice they 'fell into' rather than a reflection of societal expectations. Celebrates a complementary differentiation.

examples.text: Why do women complain when their husbands don't cook? It's a skill, and frankly, most men aren't as naturally gifted in the kitchen. My wife makes amazing food, so I'd rather her do it than us both suffer through my attempts. Practicality first. #TruthBomb #GenderRoles
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses men's cooking ability as a lack of 'natural gift,' implying women are naturally better. Justifies maintaining the traditional division ('I'd rather her do it') based on perceived practicality and a false sense of male incompetence.

examples.text: My wife keeps telling me I should learn to cook, but honestly, I'm just terrible at it, and she's so quick and efficient. I contribute by handling all the big purchases and paperwork for the house; it just makes more sense to play to our strengths.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Feigns incompetence ('terrible at it') to resist taking on traditionally female tasks, while rationalizing his existing (gendered) contributions as 'playing to strengths.' Implies her efficiency is a natural trait, not learned skill.

examples.text: (to a female colleague) "You know, my husband keeps asking me to teach him how to manage the domestic staff and daily expenses, but I told him it's better if I just handle it. It's too much detail for men, they're not cut out for all that nitty-gritty."
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman actively resists changing traditional roles by dismissing men's capacity for household management as being 'not cut out' for 'nitty-gritty,' indicating internalized misogyny and complementary gender differentiation.

examples.text: We tried a chore rotation, but after a month, we realized both of us were spending twice as long on tasks we disliked or weren't good at. So now, I do all the outdoor gardening and car maintenance, and she handles the cooking and laundry – we're both much happier and it's genuinely quicker.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: This example describes a division of labor based on actual, tested individual preferences and observed efficiency after an attempt at rotation, rather than pre-conceived gender roles. The justification is based on specific, personal experiences of skill/dislike, not generalized gender assumptions.

examples.text: My husband wanted to surprise me with dinner, but he managed to set off the smoke alarm twice and burnt the rice. Bless his effort, but I think for now, cooking is just something I'm better equipped to handle quickly after work.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: This describes a specific instance of a man's incompetence at a task, leading to a practical conclusion about who should perform it. It focuses on demonstrated individual skill (or lack thereof), rather than generalizing about men's natural inability to cook.

examples.text: Yaar, busy schedule mein kya hi karein. Meri wife aur main ne discuss kiya tha, we just decided jo jahan better hai, woh kare. Toh office ke baad I handle investments, bills, and sab, and she handles ghar ka khana, kids' homework, and all. Practical hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Describes a classic gendered division of labor (man = finance/external, woman = domestic/childcare) as a mutually decided, practical, and efficient arrangement, naturalizing the woman's sole responsibility for domestic work despite likely having a job.

examples.text: OMG, guess what? Rahul made dinner last night! Pasta! 😮 Meri to aankhein khuli reh gayi. He's such a modern husband, kitna dhyan rakhta hai mera. Bahut lucky hoon main.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses extreme surprise and effusive praise for a husband doing a basic domestic chore, implying it's an exceptional act for men and not a shared responsibility. Reinforces the low bar for male contribution and the idea that it's a 'help' not a duty.

examples.text: In most Indian dual-income households, you see a natural division. Husbands tend to focus on the 'big picture' stuff – career growth, financial planning, external investments. Wives, even if working, just seem more naturally suited to managing the 'gharelu mamle' – daily expenses, groceries, school. It's not a rule, it just evolves that way because it works best for the family structure.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames traditional gender roles in dual-income homes as a 'natural division' that 'just evolves' and 'works best,' subtly dismissing any underlying patriarchal structures and presenting it as an organic, efficient choice without coercion.

examples.text: Why should I learn to change a tire or haggle with mechanics? Honestly, I don't have the patience for it. My husband handles all vehicle-related issues, bill payments, and any 'technical' stuff. It's just more practical this way. Women are generally better at managing home, kids, and social stuff, and men at these outdoor, difficult tasks. Why complicate things?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly states a preference for gendered division of labor based on perceived natural aptitudes (women for home, men for technical/outdoor tasks), framing her own learned helplessness and adherence to traditional roles as a practical and efficient choice.

examples.text:
Speaker A: So, who usually drives when you guys go out of town? 
Speaker B: Oh, definitely me. Pooja drives locally sometimes, but for long distances or highway driving, I always take the wheel. She's not as comfortable with it, and frankly, I'm just better at it. It’s not something we decided, it just sort of happened automatically, jo jahan better hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Justifies the man always driving long distances by claiming the woman is 'not as comfortable' and he's 'better,' presenting this gendered division as an automatic, natural outcome based on competence, rather than an imposed role or lack of opportunity for the woman to develop skills.

examples.text:
Speaker A: So, how do you manage finances with your busy job, Meera? 
Speaker B: Oh, I don't! Rahul handles all our investments, taxes, and bank stuff. Woh hi better handle karte hain, I'd probably mess it up. Mujhe toh bas ghar ka kharcha aur grocery list banana aata hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman overtly expresses her learned helplessness and lack of engagement in financial planning, attributing superior capability to her husband and confining herself to domestic budgeting, framing this as a practical and natural division.

examples.text: Just helping out my wife with dinner tonight! She works so hard, so I thought I'd give her a break. Cooked a simple dal-chawal. It's important to give your partner thodi help, fellas! #HusbandDuties #ModernMan #HomeChef
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames the act of cooking a simple meal for his working wife as 'helping out' and 'giving her a break,' implying that cooking is primarily her duty and his contribution is an exceptional, commendable act, rather than a shared responsibility. Hashtags reinforce this perception.

examples.text: My friends keep telling me to learn about mutual funds and investments. But honestly, numbers aur calculations, woh mera bas ka nahi hai. My husband handles all our financial planning and investments; he's much better at it. I prefer focusing on other things, like managing the home and kids' education. To each their own!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman explicitly states disinterest and perceived inability to handle financial planning, delegating it entirely to her husband based on his presumed superior aptitude, while reinforcing her role in domestic management. This internalizes gendered division as a personal preference ('To each their own').

examples.text: Honestly, Rekha's a great team member, but for that senior client-facing role with all the travel, I don't think she's the most practical choice. She has two young kids, and let's be real, uski family ko uski ghar pe zyada zaroorat padegi. Uske husband toh bahar ka kaam hi dekhte hain.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Subtly suggests a female colleague is less suitable for a demanding client-facing role due to her family responsibilities, implying women are 'naturally' needed more at home and men handle external work, presenting it as a practical consideration for the family's needs rather than a professional capability assessment.

examples.text: You know, my colleague Priya told me her husband actually cleans the bathroom and helps with kitchen duties. I was like, 'Wow, that's amazing! Most men won't even lift a finger.' It's so rare to find such a supportive partner.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses overt surprise and high praise for a man performing basic household chores, treating it as an exceptional and rare act ('Most men won't even lift a finger') rather than a normal part of shared domestic responsibility. Reinforces the low expectation for male contribution.

examples.text: My husband is a morning person and leaves for work early, so he drops the kids off at school. I work from home and prefer cooking, so I manage dinner. We balance based on our schedules and preferences, and review it every few months.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Describes a division of labor based on individual schedules, preferences, and mutual agreement, not on inherent gender roles or perceived capabilities. Both partners are active participants in decision-making and negotiation.

examples.text: I want to surprise my wife by cooking something special this weekend. Any easy recipes for Mughlai chicken? She's always cooking for us, so I thought it's my turn to treat her.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses a desire to cook as a gesture of appreciation and reciprocation, indicating an understanding of shared responsibility and a willingness to contribute, rather than treating cooking as an exceptional male act or solely a woman's domain.

examples.text: Yaar, wife is naturally better at household planning and managing the maid. I handle all the finances and big-ticket purchases. It's just practical, jo jahan better hai, right?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames a gendered division as a 'natural' and 'practical' division of labor based on inherent strengths, not societal norms.

examples.text: My husband made chai today, so sweet! He helps out sometimes, but ghar ka kaam toh mera hi hai. He's always busy with office, I manage the home.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Celebrates a minimal male contribution while explicitly stating that household work is primarily her responsibility, reinforcing traditional gender roles.

examples.text: My wife and I both work full-time, but we just naturally fell into a system where she handles meal planning, groceries, and kids' schedules. I manage the investments and car maintenance. It’s not about gender, it's just what each of us is better at or prefers, making our lives efficient. No point forcing a 50/50 split if it's not optimal.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Describes a traditional gendered division as a 'natural' and efficient outcome of individual preferences/strengths, denying a gendered basis while maintaining it.

examples.text: Honestly, this whole 'modern husband' trend is getting out of hand. Your wife is supposed to run the house, that's her domain. You're the provider. If you want to 'help' with dishes once in a while, great, but don't pretend like cooking and cleaning is your primary responsibility. Let women manage what they're naturally better at.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly dismisses the concept of shared domestic labor, framing household management as a woman's natural domain and men's role as providing.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Pooja, tumhare husband toh kabhi kitchen mein nahi dikhte. Tum hi sab karti ho?
Speaker B: Arre, usko time kahaan milta hai? Poora din office mein rehta hai, clients ke saath. Main toh work-from-home karti hoon, so I manage the kitchen and kids. It’s practical, jo jahan better hai. Aur usko aata bhi nahi hai cooking.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: The speaker (woman) justifies her husband's lack of involvement by citing his demanding job and his 'lack of skill', reinforcing that household work is her 'practical' responsibility.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Yaar, Sameer takes care of his baby all evening now that his wife started working night shifts.
Speaker B: Wah! Modern husband! But doesn't he feel weird? Bachche toh maa hi acche se sambhaal sakti hain, na? It's just more natural. Uski wife ko hi karna chahiye tha, woh bhi night shift?
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Expresses surprise and subtle judgment at a man taking primary childcare responsibility, framing a mother's role as 'natural' and implicitly more effective.

examples.text: Instagram post with a picture of her husband changing a lightbulb: 'He fixes the broken tap, I manage the house. We just fell into these roles where we both contribute uniquely to our family's well-being. It's all about playing to your strengths, right? #TeamWork #FamilyGoals #PracticalLiving'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Presents a highly gendered division of labor as a conscious, 'practical' choice based on individual 'strengths', while subtly naturalizing traditional roles.

examples.text: Twitter: 'Why is everyone suddenly expecting men to 'help' with everything at home? It's just how we've always done it – men earn, women run the home. It’s not about superiority, it's about practical roles. Stop making it a competition, respect the natural order.'
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses calls for shared domestic labor by framing the traditional gendered division as a 'natural order' and 'practical' way things have always been.

examples.text: Mummy, woh kitchen ke shelves main kaise arrange karoon? Woh toh Rina hi jaanti hai kahan kya rakha hai, she has a system. Main toh office se thaka hua aata hoon, she manages ghar ka kaam better than me anyway.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Exhibits 'learned helplessness' and deflects responsibility for a common household task, framing it as the wife's specialized domain and his own incapacity.

examples.text: My son is busy with his career, someone has to look after the kids and cook proper meals. It's good that Aarti (daughter-in-law) works, but the home needs managing too. She's doing both, that's why she's a superwoman!
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Praises the daughter-in-law for managing both work and home, implicitly stating that domestic labor is her primary responsibility and her husband's career takes precedence.

examples.text: My husband enjoys managing our investments because he's passionate about stock market analysis, and I genuinely find cooking and baking relaxing after a long day at my job. So, we've naturally gravitated towards these tasks based on our individual interests and skills.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Division based purely on individual preference and enjoyment of specific tasks, without attributing 'natural' gendered abilities or essentializing roles. The focus is on *personal* choice, not general gender roles.

examples.text: Our domestic help is on leave this week, so my wife and I sat down, made a list of all chores, and divided them equally based on who has more flexibility in their work schedule each day. Today I'm doing laundry and dinner, she's doing kids' school prep and cleaning.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Describes an intentional, equal division of labor based on current practicalities (schedule), not on inherent gender roles or 'natural' abilities. No 'helping' framing, but shared responsibility.
